EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front moved into the region with east winds bringing in deep moisture up to about the Rio Grande Valley. Upper level flow was out of the northwest providing sufficient shear for some severe thunderstorms to develop and some isolated flash flooding. Wind gusts up to 66 mph and hail up to an inch and a half in diameter were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Las Cruces Police reported flooding in the Picacho Hills area down by the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active monsoon pattern that characterized July 2014 continued into August, with an especially wet period during the first several days of the month. Widespread heavy rain eased from the 6th through 8th, however, as more typical isolated to scattered thunderstorms dominated the landscape from the 9th through 12th.  A monsoon burst, aided by a subtropical low pressure system aloft, brought heavy rain and local flash flooding on the 13th and 14th. Drier air worked into the northwest parts of the state with much less storm activity from the 15th into the 21st. Another good surge of monsoon moisture from the south arrived late on the 21st with widespread showers and storms on the 22nd, some with heavy rain and flash flooding. The 23rd was much drier then a gradual increase in moisture returned on the 24th and continued into the 27th. The 26th was an active day with a few reports of significant flash flooding. The remainder of the month turned much drier and warmer.  Despite the active monsoon,  New Mexico's rangeland and pastures were rated 56% very poor to poor at the beginning of the month,  with  only modest improvements during the month. Despite short-term gains,  longer-term hydrological impacts (below-normal reservoir levels) remained after multiple years of below-normal snowpack. The Middle Rio Grande Conservancy District curtailed water bank deliveries for irrigation in response to low flows along the Rio Grande during the month. The combination of short- and long-term hydrological impacts, below-normal mountain snowpack conditions in the headwater regions led to the re-introduction of an area of Moderate Drought (D1) in the Middle Rio Grande corridor from Socorro County northward to Sante Fe County.

A very moist atmosphere was in place for efficient rain producing storms. An upper level trough was approaching the Four Corners region to provide enough lift for widepsread showers and thunderstorms to develop over Sierra and Dona Ana Counties. Over two inches of rain was reported near the Spaceport in just over one hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Spaceport official reported over 2 inches of rain falling in about 80 minutes. The main road south of the Spaceport was washed out and closed for approximately 3 days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A majority of central and northern New Mexico received below normal precipitation during April 2014.  As a result,  severe (D2) or worse drought conditions expanded areawide and worsened for parts of the far east, central and west.  An estimated 86 percent of the state was classified in severe drought or worse at the end of April,  as compared to 65 per cent at the beginning of the month.  Exceptional drought (D4) returned to far eastern New Mexico from the Clovis area north to Tucumcari and Clayton.

Near normal rainfall occurred across southwestern New Mexico with Hidalgo County being taken out of the Severe(D2) drought and put into Moderate(D1) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southwestern up to the northeastern portions of the zone started in Severe(D2) drought but were put into Moderate(D1) drought on October 21st. The Cooperative Observer at Antelope Wells reported 0.61 inches of precipitation which was 0.01 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near normal rainfall occurred across southwestern New Mexico with Hidalgo County being taken out of the Severe(D2) drought and put into Moderate(D1) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The eastern portions of the zone started the month in Severe(D2) drought but on October 21st were placed in Moderate(D1) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was dropping south through New Mexico with an upper level trough moving across the Rockies. These two features combined to create a moderately unstable atmosphere with one stronger thunderstorm producing hail up to the size of dimes and moderate to heavy rain.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the unseasonably deep upper level storm system shifted east across northern New Mexico on the 27th, strong winds and areas of blowing dust redeveloped over much of the area.  West to northwest winds were the strongest along and east of the central mountain chain where numerous locations reported peak wind gusts of around 60 to 65 mph.  No significant damage was reported on this second day either. Areas of blowing dust occurred early in the day across far eastern New Mexico before pushing east into Texas.  Showers and colder air across northern and western New Mexico produced gusty winds in those areas again however speeds were lighter than on the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Angel Fire AWOS hit 46 mph sustained with a peak gust to 58 mph during the early morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was a cold front near the Pecos River on the eleventh which provided a source of convergence and lift.  This front shifted north over the central and northern Permian Basin as well as southeast New Mexico that night.  The atmosphere was very moist and unstable.  There were weak winds aloft which allowed for slower moving storms and rain contributing to flash flooding.  Another cold front moved through the area on the twelfth.  Upper level disturbances were also over the region which allowed for more atmospheric lift.  These conditions combined to produce flash flooding across southeast New Mexico and West Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Eddy County and produced flash flooding across the county.  The Eddy County Commissioner reported that numerous roads were closed in Eddy County.  The extent of damage is unknown so the cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge dominated the upper air pattern over the Western United States with weak flow over the Borderland. A surface convergence area was setup near the Rio Grande Valley with sufficient moisture in place to produce thunderstorms across the region. One of these storms produced a quick inch of rain around Hill which lead to some localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over an inch of rain fell quickly in Hill, NM which caused the closing of Dona Ana Road which was underwater. Several houses had water in them, plus damage to trucks and a horse trailer were also reported. Water made it into the basement of the historic St. Mary's at Hill Anglican Church. One resident said that the water was higher than any other time in the 50 years he lived there.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge dominated the upper air pattern over the Western United States with a weak disturbance moving around it. A surface convergence area was setup near the Arizona border with dew points around 60 all of the way into southwest New Mexico. The disturbance combined with the moisture in place to help develop thunderstorms with locally heavy rain across the Gila Region into the Blacks Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The New Mexico Department of Transportation reported some water crossing the road on New Mexico Highway 52 near mile post 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary was setup near the Rio Grande Valley with easterly surface winds bringing dew points around 60F to the area. Upper level flow was out of the northwest on the back side of a trough which provided sufficient shear for isolated severe thunderstorms to develop. Numerous trees and power lines were reported down in Alamogordo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of trees down across the county from winds. Car windows were broken and a roof was blown off a trailer home in Alamogordo. A transformer fell into a tree and the tree then fell into a house. About 2000 people in the area were without power for several hours. Some locally dense blowing dust was also reported with the storm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in San Lorenzo reported 1 inch of rain in about an hour with some street flooding;.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remains of Tropical Storm Odile were over the region.  The atmosphere was very moist and there was a mid-level circulation over northern portions of the area.  There were nocturnal low level winds that produced additional lift which aided in more rain and thunderstorms developing.  The saturated ground from previous rains allowed for flash flooding to develop across West Texas and southeast New Mexico when additional rain moved over these same locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Eddy County and produced flash flooding southeast of Whites City.  The National Guard was continuing to provide assistance in getting supplies to, and extracting, a 48 person work crew that sheltered in place overnight south of Whites City Road due to rising flood waters.  The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

An estimated 86 percent of the state was classified in severe drought or worse at the end of April,  as compared to 65 per cent at the beginning of the month.  Exceptional drought (D4) returned to far eastern New Mexico from the Clovis area north to Tucumcari and Clayton.

An active monsoon pattern that characterized July 2014 continued into August, with an especially wet period during the first several days of the month. Widespread heavy rain eased from the 6th through 8th, however, as more typical isolated to scattered thunderstorms dominated the landscape from the 9th through 12th.  A monsoon burst, aided by a subtropical low pressure system aloft, brought heavy rain and local flash flooding on the 13th and 14th. Drier air worked into the northwest parts of the state with much less storm activity from the 15th into the 21st. Another good surge of monsoon moisture from the south arrived late on the 21st with widespread showers and storms on the 22nd, some with heavy rain and flash flooding. The 23rd was much drier then a gradual increase in moisture returned on the 24th and continued into the 27th. The 26th was an active day with a few reports of significant flash flooding. The remainder of the month turned much drier and warmer.  Despite the active monsoon,  New Mexico's rangeland and pastures were rated 56% very poor to poor at the beginning of the month,  with  only modest improvements during the month. Despite short-term gains,  longer-term hydrological impacts (below-normal reservoir levels) remained after multiple years of below-normal snowpack. The Middle Rio Grande Conservancy District curtailed water bank deliveries for irrigation in response to low flows along the Rio Grande during the month. The combination of short- and long-term hydrological impacts, below-normal mountain snowpack conditions in the headwater regions led to the re-introduction of an area of Moderate Drought (D1) in the Middle Rio Grande corridor from Socorro County northward to Sante Fe County.

A weak surface low was located just north of Truth or Consequences with an upper level trough approaching from the west. Some drier air moved into the region with dew points in the upper 40s. Outflow from a decaying thunderstorm moved through and produced a wind gust to 59 mph at the Truth or Consequences Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Truth or Consequences ASOS reported a peak wind gust of 59 mph from a thunderstorm outflow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture continued to remain in place across the region with a weak upper trough crossing the Rockies providing lift to the area. Heavy rain fell on the Silver Burn Scar and around Kingston which caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 2.25 inches of rain fell in the Kingston area and over the Silver Burn Scar. A total of 2.25 inches of rain was reported in Kingston which led to numerous county roads being flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow from storms that died north of Silver City brought strong winds to the Santa Clara area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Silver City Sun News reported that two large trees fell on vehicles in Santa Clara with one of them possibly totaled. One of the vehicles was a Hummer. Recent heavy rainfall likely saturated the ground around the trees prior to the event. No thunderstorm affected the Santa Clara area, but outflow from dying storms to the north moved through the area. The peak gust reported at the Silver City Airport was 46 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance was approaching the area out of Southern Arizona with modest moisture and instability in place over Southwestern New Mexico. Slow moving thunderstorms brought over an inch of rain to Hachita which created some minor flooding issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cooperative Observer reported 1.12 inches of rain fell quickly in Hachita and caused some street flooding in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist atmosphere was in place for efficient rain producing storms. An upper level trough was approaching the Four Corners region to provide enough lift for widepsread showers and thunderstorms to develop over Sierra and Dona Ana Counties. Over two inches of rain was reported near the Spaceport in just over one hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several homes were flooded in Rodey on Mariano and Trujillo Streets as well as on Colorado Street.

A moderately moist atmosphere was in place with a weak upper disturbance crossing the region. Weak low and mid level flow kept storm motion very slow allowing for locally heavy rain to develop over Southern Dona Ana County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 1.55 inches of rain fell in Santa Teresa with most of the rain falling in 20 minutes. Small hail was also reported. Street flooding was reported with water over the curb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was a cold front near the Pecos River on the eleventh which provided a source of convergence and lift.  This front shifted north over the central and northern Permian Basin as well as southeast New Mexico that night.  The atmosphere was very moist and unstable.  There were weak winds aloft which allowed for slower moving storms and rain contributing to flash flooding.  Another cold front moved through the area on the twelfth.  Upper level disturbances were also over the region which allowed for more atmospheric lift.  These conditions combined to produce flash flooding across southeast New Mexico and West Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain moved across Eddy County and produced flash flooding near Artesia.  A storm spotter reported one foot of water running over U.S. 285 north of Artesia.  The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

Typical monsoon moisture over the region resulted in some weak showers and thunderstorms to develop.  The initial lightning strike from a storm in Las Cruces resulted in 6 people being injured during football practice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six people, including several middle school football players were injured from the initial lightning strike from a nearby thunderstorm. No fatalities occurred with this lightning strike, but one student who was closest to the strike suffered significant injuries and was in the hospital for over a week.

An upper ridge was centered over West Texas with a surface trough across extreme West Texas/eastern New Mexico.  There was intense heating along this trough with a weak upper level disturbance over the region which increased lift.  Upper level winds were weak and there was abundant moisture which aided in the development of heavy rain.  These conditions also contributed to large hail in southeast New Mexico.

Low level east to southeast flow started bringing in some low level moisture to the region with weak flow in the mid levels. Storms developed over the Sacramento Mountains and moved west onto the lowlands toward Tularosa which caused some localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported around the Riata Road area west of Tularosa where water was crossing the road. Drainage ditches along Highway 54 were also filling up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual moisture from Hurricane Odile was getting trapped under a building upper ridge across the Rockies. A weak upper level disturbance came down the east side of this ridge and triggered showers and thunderstorms over the western Sacramento Mountains. Slow storm motion combined with the plentiful moisture led to locally heavy rain and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 82 was closed just west of the tunnel to Cloudcroft was closed due to a rock slide. A section of road at La Luz Creek crossing was closed as well due to flooding.

Another good surge of monsoon moisture from the south arrived late on the 21st with widespread showers and storms on the 22nd, some with heavy rain and flash flooding. The 23rd was much drier then a gradual increase in moisture returned on the 24th and continued into the 27th. The 26th was an active day with a few reports of significant flash flooding. The remainder of the month turned much drier and warmer.  Despite the active monsoon,  New Mexico's rangeland and pastures were rated 56% very poor to poor at the beginning of the month,  with  only modest improvements during the month. Despite short-term gains,  longer-term hydrological impacts (below-normal reservoir levels) remained after multiple years of below-normal snowpack. The Middle Rio Grande Conservancy District curtailed water bank deliveries for irrigation in response to low flows along the Rio Grande during the month. The combination of short- and long-term hydrological impacts, below-normal mountain snowpack conditions in the headwater regions led to the re-introduction of an area of Moderate Drought (D1) in the Middle Rio Grande corridor from Socorro County northward to Sante Fe County.

A majority of central and northern New Mexico received below normal precipitation during April 2014.  As a result,  severe (D2) or worse drought conditions expanded areawide and worsened for parts of the far east, central and west.  An estimated 86 percent of the state was classified in severe drought or worse at the end of April,  as compared to 65 per cent at the beginning of the month.  Exceptional drought (D4) returned to far eastern New Mexico from the Clovis area north to Tucumcari and Clayton.

An upper level disturbance was moving over the Oklahoma and Texas panhandles.  A cold front associated with this disturbance was moving toward the area with a surface trough present ahead of the front.  Intense heating was being experienced along the surface trough which contributed to good instability.  The upper high over the region was moving back to the west which helped increase lift over the area.  These conditions contributed to large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding across southeast New Mexico.

There was a cold front near the Pecos River on the eleventh which provided a source of convergence and lift.  This front shifted north over the central and northern Permian Basin as well as southeast New Mexico that night.  The atmosphere was very moist and unstable.  There were weak winds aloft which allowed for slower moving storms and rain contributing to flash flooding.  Another cold front moved through the area on the twelfth.  Upper level disturbances were also over the region which allowed for more atmospheric lift.  These conditions combined to produce flash flooding across southeast New Mexico and West Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Lea County and produced wind damage in Eunice.  The Eunice Police Department reported trees blown down in town.  The cost of damage is a rough estimate.

A back door cold front was located near the Rio Grande Valley with an upper level trough approaching from the west. Moisture had entered the region from the east on the back side of the front and ample instability provided fuel for thunderstorms to develop across the region. A peak wind gust of 69 mph was reported at the NWS Office in Santa Teresa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several gusts to near 60 knots occurred at the NWS Office in Santa Teresa with a peak gust of 69 mph. Visibility was also reduced to near zero in blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most areas received near to above average precipitation during the month of August.  Much of the Sacramento Mountains by the end of the month were completely out of drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The zone started with Severe(D2) drought and transitioned to Moderate(D1) drought on August 5th. The Mountain Park Cooperative Observer reported 3.69 inches of precipitation which was 0.03 inches above normal.

Strong southwest flow ahead of a large-scale upper level storm system to the west of New Mexico produced gusty winds across much of the state.  Spotty strong winds were reported around the Gallup area.  A peak wind of 60mph was reported at the site along with high sustained wind speeds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds between 41 and 43 mph for two hours with a gust to 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Phillips Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist atmosphere was in place with weak flow aloft keeping storm motion very slow. A weak upper level trough was passing over the region which triggered scattered thunderstorms with very heavy rain which lead to over 20 houses in Alamogordo getting flooded and mud slides south of Las Cruces.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported that numerous streets in Alamogordo were flooded. Between 20 to 30 homes on the north side of town were flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NMDOT reported mudslides and debris on Frontage Roads 1037 and 1035 between Las Cruces and Vado from overnight rains. Several buildings on the New Mexico State University Campus  reported water inside of them, including two rooms in the Health and Social Sciences Building, heavy water in east gymnasium of Activity Center and several Engineering Complex buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the Great Basin raced across New Mexico on the 13th.  Enough moisture and forcing along the frontal boundary combined to produce intense virga showers across many areas.  Powerful wind gusts were reported at numerous locations, including 67 mph at Gallup and Santa Fe.  A large tree was blown over near San Antonio as the cold front pushed through the area.  Meanwhile, gusty winds also impacted isolated areas of eastern New Mexico as the back door front plowed south into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry showers produced intense wind gusts up to 67 mph.  Blowing dust reduced the visibility down to 2 miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active spring weather scenario unfolded across northern and central New Mexico on the 5th and 6th as gusty winds, mountain snow, and valley showers and thunderstorms impacted the region. Unstable northwest flow and sufficient moisture over the region produced persistent snow showers over the northern high terrain. Seven to 9 inches of fresh snow was reported around Amalia, Red River and Taos Ski Valley.  A man photographing a dust devil near Moriarty was grazed by a lightning strike from a dry thunderstorm.  No reports of high winds were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was grazed by a lightning strike 10 feet away while photographing a dust devil near Moriarty.  The man survived the shock however no details on the extent of his injuries was reported.

Precipitation varied greatly across the area with some locations receiving over 200% of normal while others areas were slightly below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme(D3) drought was reported for the entire zone except the far north where Severe(D2) drought was noted. The Jornada Range Cooperative Observer reported 1.89 inches of precipitation which was only 0.02 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough was moving through Arizona with a weak ridge over the front range of the Rockies. At the surface, a bit of a dryline was located near the Rio Grande Valley providing some low level convergence for storms to develop. Residual moisture from heavy rains earlier in the week contributed to the locally heavy rain that developed around the Columbus area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two homes along Highway 11 were evacuated due to inundation with at least one inaccessible overnight. Water crossed Highway 11 near mile marker 10 where one car got stuck. The first few arroyos along Highway 9 west of Columbus flooded due to runoff from heavy rain over the Tres Hermanas Mountains. Total rainfall just north of Columbus was 2.97 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near normal rainfall occurred again during October over the Gila Region with the western portions of Grant County the only areas where drought conditions continued through the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone remained in Severe(D2) drought through the month of October. The Cooperative Observer at Gila Hot Springs reported 1.56 inches of rain which was 0.08 inches above normal.

Strong upper level flow continued to impact the region as the potent upper level storm system remained nearly stationary over the central plains states.  Northwest winds were the strongest along and east of the central mountain chain from Clines Corners to Tucumcari and Clovis where numerous locations reported peak wind gusts of 60 to 67 mph. No widespread significant damage was reported on this third day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four west southwest of Tucumcari at Union Pacific Rail site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the unseasonably deep upper level storm system shifted east across northern New Mexico on the 27th, strong winds and areas of blowing dust redeveloped over much of the area.  West to northwest winds were the strongest along and east of the central mountain chain where numerous locations reported peak wind gusts of around 60 to 65 mph.  No significant damage was reported on this second day either. Areas of blowing dust occurred early in the day across far eastern New Mexico before pushing east into Texas.  Showers and colder air across northern and western New Mexico produced gusty winds in those areas again however speeds were lighter than on the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Moriarty AWOS hit 43 mph sustained during the early morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system strengthened as it approached from California and swept through north central New Mexico. A subtropical moisture tap in association with this system produced heavy snow across the northern mountains on the 13th. Additional snow fell as the system exited on the 14th due to wrap around moisture, resulting in snowfall storm totals of up to 20 inches in the Sangre de Cristo Mountains.  Strong mid level winds and lee side trough quickly shifted the focus to high  winds on the 14th, with the strongest reports across the east central plains.  Numerous hours with sustained winds of 40 mph along with gusts up to 60 mph were seen across the region during the late morning and early afternoon hours on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KCVS ASOS reported peak wind gust of 60 mph and sustained wind speeds of 40 mph. KCVN AWOS also reported sustained wind speeds of 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system shifted eastward from the Great Basin.  The associated strong jet stream and weak moisture plume created widespread gusty winds. Winds were strongest in typical northwest flow areas like the Sandia and Manzano Mountains eastward to Clines Corners where gusts near 60 mph were reported. Light snowfall amounts were reported in many areas along and east of the central mountain chain, however the heaviest accumulations were over far northeast New Mexico on the back side of the exiting storm system. Generally 5 to 10 inches was reported over northern Colfax and Union counties. Several highways were then closed over the northeast plains due to blowing snow as the storm intensified over the Great Plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Los Alamos airport and nearby Mesonet sites report gusts in excess of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shallow moist, arctic air mass firmly entrenched over eastern New Mexico combined with deep moist southwest flow over the area created widespread freezing drizzle and very light snow. The combination of frigid temperatures and light frozen or freezing precipitation created significant impacts to travel over southeastern New Mexico. Many state and U.S. highways were closed for several hours due to black ice and extremely dangerous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle and light snow produced major interruptions to travel over the area. NM highway 206 was closed for several hours between Portales and Tatum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moving over the region resulted in high winds over the Guadalupe Mountains.

January 2014 was exceptionally dry and needless to say much drier than normal while temperatures were near to above normal.   Average statewide precipitation during the month was a dismal 0.03 inches, which was the driest January on record.  Drought conditions steadily worsened as the month progressed.   Nearly half (48%) of the state was classified in severe or worse (D2-D4) drought at the end of the month,  roughly a 15 percent increase from the beginning of the year.

An upper level trough skirted the northern border with lee side troughing at the surface. Strong northwest winds developed during the morning across the central highlands as the wave moved east and daytime heating and mixing began. Winds had already begun to pick up during the overnight hours producing some mountain wave activity along the Sangre de Cristos and suspected at the Sandia and Manzanos as well. Wind speeds peaked in the afternoon with peak gusts nearing 60 mph. The northwesterly winds gave way to a strong back door cold front during the late afternoon. The front quickly plunged southward through the eastern plains continuing the high winds through the evening. Wind speeds gradually decreased into Wednesday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CSBF Fort Sumner airport recorded a peak gust to 58 mph during the evening of the 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent area of low clouds and widespread dense fog over southeastern New Mexico on December 8th resulted in one motor vehicle fatality in Chaves County. Media reports indicated a 28 year old man from Roswell was killed when his car crashed into a semi-truck on Highway 380, near mile post 196 (approximately 6 miles west of Caprock, or 41 miles east of Roswell), in dense fog. Three other passengers in the vehicle were taken to the hospital.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reports indicated a 28 year old man from Roswell was killed when his car crashed into a semi-truck on Highway 380, near mile post 196 (approximately 6 miles west of Caprock, or 41 miles east of Roswell), in dense fog. Three other passengers in the vehicle were taken to the hospital. The accident occurred at 630am MST on Monday, December 8th. A semi-truck driver was in the wrong lane as he was driving into a rest stop. As the truck driver attempted to move back into the correct lane, the passenger vehicle crashed into the back end of the trailer, which was still in the wrong lane.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving upper level storm system shifted southeast out of Colorado into the southern Rockies of New Mexico. Widespread windy west winds over much of the region turned northwest throughout the day as cold air advanced into the area. The strongest wind gusts were found in the area from Albuquerque south and east to Clines Corners and Chaves County. A deep fetch of cold, moist, and unstable air behind the trough produced heavy snowfall over portions of the northern high terrain. The Sangre de Cristo Mountains were particularly hard hit. A band of snow developed over Taos County and repeatedly impacted the area from near Taos to Tres Ritos, Santa Fe Ski Basin, Gascon, and even onto the Interstate 25 corridor from Raton to Las Vegas. Upwards of 6 to 13 inches of snow was reported, particularly in the higher elevation areas. Widespread difficult travel conditions were reported across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOTELs, CoCoRaHS, and ski sites reported between 4 and 13 inches of snow across the area, with the heaviest amounts in the area around Tres Ritos and Truchas. Widespread difficult driving conditions were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow dominated the upper level weather pattern as a trough continued to move east into Texas. At the surface a high pressure system built over western Colorado and a surface low deepened into north Texas. An associated cold front with the strong northwest winds aloft led to breezy to windy conditions across the central mountain chain and into the adjacent eastern plains by late morning lasting into the evening hours. Most locations stayed within wind advisory criteria with a handful hitting high wind speeds to the lee of the mountains and southeastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at CQC recorded high sustained winds speed of 45mph along with wind gusts ranging from 54mph to 61mph during the afternoon and early evening hours of January 10, 2014.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry and windy westerly flow developed across the Middle Rio Grande Valley ahead of an approaching cold front.  The 11-acre fire dubbed the Lucky 7 Fire began about 2:40 p.m. off Gabaldon Road in Los Chavez west of the Rio Grande.  It burned a shed and threatened several homes as it spread driven by gusty winds. By late evening the fire was considered 50 percent contained with little activity on the perimeter although hot spots fanned by the wind continued to smolder in the interior.  Valencia County firefighters supported by State Forestry and U.S. Fish and Wildlife crews fought the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 11-acre Lucky 7 Fire began about 2:40 p.m. off Gabaldon Road in Los Chavez west of the Rio Grande. It burned a shed and threatened several homes as it spread driven by gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system over Utah shifted slowly east southeast across New Mexico on the 15th and 16th. Meanwhile a powerful back door cold front moved south and west across the eastern plains, ushering in the coldest temperatures of the fall season. The combination of cold air, sufficient moisture, and upslope flow along the central mountain chain produced hefty snowfall accumulations. Upwards of 12 to 18 inches were reported in portions of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains, with roughly 1 to 5 inches over much of the eastern plains. No major impacts to travel were noted along interstate corridors, however U.S. Highway 54 was closed within Quay County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters, SNOTELs, and COOP sites reported between 6 and 9 inches of snow. No major impacts to travel were noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mix of freezing rain, sleet and snow fell over southeast New Mexico and west Texas as an arctic airmass resided over the region, and downstream of a slow moving upper trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads have been closed in New Mexico due to ice and snow. This includes U.S. Highway 82 from Artesia to Cloudcroft.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice and snow accumulations resulted in road closures in New Mexico, including U.S. Highway 82 from Lovington to the Texas State line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, slow-moving upper level storm system crossed central and northern New Mexico Christmas night through the 26th. Areas of snow initially set up over northwest and west-central New Mexico late Christmas day along the associated Pacific front. As the cold front slowly moved eastward, bands of heavy snow developed and quickly expanded resulting in widespread, moderate to significant accumulations for the higher elevations of central and northern New Mexico as well as the east-central and northeast high plains. Meanwhile, a back door cold frontal boundary shifted southwest over the northeast plains and stalled along the east slopes of the central mountain chain. The combination of strong lift with the upper low and upslope flow along the front produced heavy snowfall accumulations for the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. Many areas in the high terrain reported 5 to 11 inches. Moderate snowfall accumulations of 3 to 6 inches were also reported along the Interstate 40 corridor around the Sandia Mountains and the Continental Divide as well as near Clines Corners and Vaughn.   Though late in the day, many areas experienced a true White Christmas if only for a few hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained Spotter and CoCoRaHS reports across the area indicated 3 to 6 inches of snowfall.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, slow-moving upper level storm system crossed central and northern New Mexico Christmas night through the 26th. Areas of snow initially set up over northwest and west-central New Mexico late Christmas day along the associated Pacific front. As the cold front slowly moved eastward, bands of heavy snow developed and quickly expanded resulting in widespread, moderate to significant accumulations for the higher elevations of central and northern New Mexico as well as the east-central and northeast high plains. Meanwhile, a back door cold frontal boundary shifted southwest over the northeast plains and stalled along the east slopes of the central mountain chain. The combination of strong lift with the upper low and upslope flow along the front produced heavy snowfall accumulations for the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. Many areas in the high terrain reported 5 to 11 inches. Moderate snowfall accumulations of 3 to 6 inches were also reported along the Interstate 40 corridor around the Sandia Mountains and the Continental Divide as well as near Clines Corners and Vaughn.   Though late in the day, many areas experienced a true White Christmas if only for a few hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP, CoCoRaHS and SNOTEL reports across the area indicated 4 to 8 inches of snowfall.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system over Utah shifted slowly east southeast across New Mexico on the 15th and 16th. Meanwhile a powerful back door cold front moved south and west across the eastern plains, ushering in the coldest temperatures of the fall season. The combination of cold air, sufficient moisture, and upslope flow along the central mountain chain produced hefty snowfall accumulations. Upwards of 12 to 18 inches were reported in portions of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains, with roughly 1 to 5 inches over much of the eastern plains. No major impacts to travel were noted along interstate corridors, however U.S. Highway 54 was closed within Quay County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of between 3 and 12 inches of snow from social media, CoCoRaHS, and SNOTELs. The heaviest amounts fell along U.S. Highway 64 between Taos and Angel Fire. Difficult driving conditions were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough dove southeastward through northeast New Mexico throughout the day. Continued tight surface pressure gradient between high pressure over southwest Colorado and low pressure trough to the east resulted in strong winds across central and eastern New Mexico. Winds aloft steadily strengthened throughout the morning into the early afternoon gradually mixing down to the surface. Wind speeds peaked during the late afternoon hours before decreasing into the late evening hours. Along with significant sustained wind speeds and wind gusts, blowing dust occurred at some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tucumcari ASOS recorded a wind gust to 58mph during the early afternoon hours of January 12, 2014.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms with strong winds, tornadoes, large hail, and heavy rainfall impacted New Mexico on this third in a series of significant severe weather days.  Thunderstorms developed along the central mountain chain early in the afternoon then moved slowly east southeast across the plains.  Several small short-lived tornadoes developed along the high plains, in particular around Maxwell, Wagon Mound, Valmora, Springer, and Encinoso.  The Encinoso tornado destroyed 2 homes and severely damaged 2 others.  Many reports of quarter to baseball size hail were also reported with these storms.  The largest hail reported was around 3 inches in diameter east of Interstate 25 near Valmora.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorm near Encinoso spawned a tornado along Highway 246 at Richardson Canyon Road.  The tornado traveled east-southeast down Richardson Canyon Road, snapping off the tops of trees and breaking numerous branches of Ponderosa pine. The tornado intensified as it approached 2 homes.  A single wide mobile home from the 1970s was completely destroyed as the undercarriage was moved about 20 feet and all walls separated and tossed. A double wide mobile home was tossed into a barn and destroyed.  Portions of the roof were blown 250 yards away.  At least two other homes as well as a barn and shed sustained damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation was below normal in the west, mostly near normal in central areas and generally above normal in the east especially the far southeast.  Approximately 28 percent of western and northern New Mexico was classified in severe drought or worse during the month, with little significant change from late October.  The northwest plateau and highlands continued to experience the most significant and persistent drought conditions while south-central and southeast areas of the state were classified as abnormally dry if not better.
Near average precipitation occurred in the month of March, but the prolonged period of below normal amounts led to an increase in drought coverage and intensity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone remained in Severe(D2) drought through the month of March. The Cooperative Observer at Gila Hot Springs reported 0.83 inches of precipitation which was 0.05 inches above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 2014 was warmer than normal over most of New Mexico. Precipitation was quite varied from non-existent to well below normal in the west to above normal in the east, with central areas having a mixture of near and below normal precipitation. Overall, precipitation shortfalls and resultant soil moisture deficits led to a small expansion of extreme drought conditions over far northeast New Mexico.  Elsewhere, despite pockets of heavy thunderstorm rainfall,  very little change in drought status was observed.

March 2014 was the most active month year-to-date, with two widespread, significant precipitation events, but overall precipitation was below average areawide. Short-term precipitation deficits and dry soil conditions led to expansion of Extreme Drought (D3) in south-central New Mexico and an expansion of Moderate Drought (D1) in southeastern New Mexico.  Otherwise,  the percent of New Mexico in severe or worse drought remained virtually unchanged from the previous month around 66 percent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 2014 was warmer than normal over most of New Mexico. Precipitation was quite varied from non-existent to well below normal in the west to above normal in the east, with central areas having a mixture of near and below normal precipitation. Overall, precipitation shortfalls and resultant soil moisture deficits led to a small expansion of extreme drought conditions over far northeast New Mexico.  Elsewhere, despite pockets of heavy thunderstorm rainfall,  very little change in drought status was observed.

Precipitation this month was varied with generally above normal values in the northwest and below normal across the east and south.  A trend of continuing dryness/drought prompted an expansion of abnormal dryness (D0) in southeast areas of the state to include Chaves, De Baca, and Roosevelt counties. Moderate drought (D1) expanded in west-central portions of the state from eastern Cibola County to the Rio Grande Valley, while severe drought (D2) expanded in northeast New Mexico. In northwest New Mexico, extreme drought (D3) was expanded slightly eastward into more of both San Juan and McKinley counties.   The first significant storm system of the month arrived late on the 3rd and continued through the 4th with widespread rain and high mountain snow. Mostly quiet and mild weather followed from the 5th through 12th. Another round of rain and snow followed from the 13th into the 14th and 16th through 17th, but amounts were mostly light. A stronger system delivered moderate snow amounts to northern and eastern New Mexico on the 22nd into the 23rd. Another storm on the 25th through 26th brought widespread light to moderate snowfall to northern and central areas of the state. A blast of arctic air arrived on the 29th followed by an approaching significant storm on New Years Eve.

March 2014 was the most active month year-to-date, with two widespread, significant precipitation events, but overall precipitation was below average areawide. Short-term precipitation deficits and dry soil conditions led to expansion of Extreme Drought (D3) in south-central New Mexico and an expansion of Moderate Drought (D1) in southeastern New Mexico.  Otherwise,  the percent of New Mexico in severe or worse drought remained virtually unchanged from the previous month around 66 percent.

A deep surge of low level moisture westward into central and eastern New Mexico interacted with an approaching upper level storm system from the Great Basin to produce another round of severe weather for the eastern plains.  Thunderstorms developed along a line over Socorro, Torrance, and Lincoln counties then advanced eastward into a bow echo over much of the plains.  The main impact from these storms was high winds.  Areas from Tucumcari south to Clovis reported peak winds between 60 and 70 mph.  No damage was reported.  Heavy rain and frequent lightning strikes accompanied these storms across the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gust to 60 mph with dime size hail reported in Tucumcari.

A slow-moving and unseasonably deep upper level trough positioned over the Great Basin combined with a surface lee trough over eastern New Mexico to produce widespread windy conditions.  Winds increased by late morning over the entire state with most areas experiencing peak winds around 45 to 50 mph. A couple of the more wind prone locations such as Gallup and Clines Corners reported peak winds near 60 mph.  No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gallup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late season breezy to windy conditions impacted much of central and eastern New Mexico once again on the 16th.  While most locations experienced peak winds in the 40 to 50 mph range, a locally stronger gust to 58 mph impacted the Gallup area around 4pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gallup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated virga shower that passed over Clines Corners around 315pm produced a locally strong wind gust up to 61 mph.  No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clines Corners.

Strong thunderstorms fired along a dryline over eastern New Mexico during the early afternoon hours of the 19th.  Several of these storms strengthened as they moved east into deeper moisture and produced severe hail over many areas of Curry and Roosevelt counties. Nickel to golf ball size hail was reported from around Clovis and Pleasant Hill south to Portales and Rogers.  No significant damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter size hail northeast of Portales.

An upper level disturbance brought breezy to windy conditions to areas in northern and central New Mexico. As the shortwave trough moved across the state, daytime heating allowed for good mixing of the boundary layer with strong winds aloft being transported to the surface.  Sustained wind speeds were generally below 30 mph with gusts into the 40s and 50s at some locations. Into the mid afternoon hours the ASOS at Clines Corners recorded a peak gust to 58 mph, this was the highest recorded for the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Clines Corners recorded gusts ranging from 49 to 58 mph during the late morning hours into the afternoon of June 27, 2014.

Severe or worse drought covered nearly two-thirds of New Mexico,  an increase of nearly 15 percent as compared to the previous month.

June 2014 was warmer than normal over most of New Mexico. Precipitation was quite varied from non-existent to well below normal in the west to above normal in the east, with central areas having a mixture of near and below normal precipitation. Overall, precipitation shortfalls and resultant soil moisture deficits led to a small expansion of extreme drought conditions over far northeast New Mexico.  Elsewhere, despite pockets of heavy thunderstorm rainfall,  very little change in drought status was observed.

June 2014 was warmer than normal over most of New Mexico. Precipitation this month was varied with generally above normal values in the northwest and below normal across the east and south.  A trend of continuing dryness/drought prompted an expansion of abnormal dryness (D0) in southeast areas of the state to include Chaves, De Baca, and Roosevelt counties. Moderate drought (D1) expanded in west-central portions of the state from eastern Cibola County to the Rio Grande Valley, while severe drought (D2) expanded in northeast New Mexico. In northwest New Mexico, extreme drought (D3) was expanded slightly eastward into more of both San Juan and McKinley counties.   The first significant storm system of the month arrived late on the 3rd and continued through the 4th with widespread rain and high mountain snow. Mostly quiet and mild weather followed from the 5th through 12th. Another round of rain and snow followed from the 13th into the 14th and 16th through 17th, but amounts were mostly light. A stronger system delivered moderate snow amounts to northern and eastern New Mexico on the 22nd into the 23rd. Another storm on the 25th through 26th brought widespread light to moderate snowfall to northern and central areas of the state. A blast of arctic air arrived on the 29th followed by an approaching significant storm on New Years Eve.

January 2014 was exceptionally dry and needless to say much drier than normal while temperatures were near to above normal.   Average statewide precipitation during the month was a dismal 0.03 inches, which was the driest January on record.  Drought conditions steadily worsened as the month progressed.   Nearly half (48%) of the state was classified in severe or worse (D2-D4) drought at the end of the month,  roughly a 15 percent increase from the beginning of the year.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late-season upper low moved into the Four Corners region with a 110+ knot jet south of it. A surface cold front accompanied the system and winds ahead of the front peaked at 81 mph at San Augustin Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Twin Peaks Mesonet site recorded a peak wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late-season upper low moved into the Four Corners region with a 110+ knot jet south of it. A surface cold front accompanied the system and winds ahead of the front peaked at 81 mph at San Augustin Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The strongest gust across the entire zone was 64 mph at Salinas Peak. Other gusts included 60 mph 20 miles west-northwest of Tularosa and 59 mph 11 miles southwest of Northrup Strip.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late-season upper low moved into the Four Corners region with a 110+ knot jet south of it. A surface cold front accompanied the system and winds ahead of the front peaked at 81 mph at San Augustin Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gust of 81 mph occurred at San Augustin Pass. Other high wind events reported included 62 mph 1 mile west of the White Sands Missile Range Main Post, 61 mph 1 mile southeast of the Main Post, 60 mph 5 miles northeast of San Augustin Pass and 59 mph 18 miles northeast of the Main Post.

Nearly half (48%) of the state was classified in severe or worse (D2-D4) drought at the end of the month,  roughly a 15 percent increase from the beginning of the year.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: February 2014 was the 9th driest on record for New Mexico, as a predominately dry northwest flow pattern prevailed statewide.  As a result, significant long- and short-term drought intensified across New Mexico. Reservoir storage was barely just over half of normal.  Severe or worse drought covered nearly two-thirds of New Mexico,  an increase of nearly 15 percent as compared to the previous month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent spring storm system crossed New Mexico and battered much of the area with strong winds, blowing dust, and mainly dry showers.  A cold frontal boundary slammed through western New Mexico during the afternoon hours and produced many areas of strong winds right along and immediately behind the front.  Just enough moisture and instability was in place for central and eastern New Mexico to produce scattered virga showers ahead of the front.  These showers exacerbated the windy conditions and pulled stronger winds aloft to the surface.  A recreation center in Las Vegas suffered roof damage as a 67 mph wind gust slammed the area.  Many other locales also reported wind gusts between 58 and 67 mph throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clines Corners peak winds likely exacerbated by some virga in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough was present to the west of the region with southeast winds bringing in good moisture across southeast New Mexico and West Texas.  Upper level disturbances moved over the Southern Plains providing lift.  A weak dryline was across the area providing a focus for thunderstorms, and a front was moving south toward the area from storms in the panhandle.  Also, winds were changing direction and increasing with height which allowed for storms to become severe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: July 2014 was wetter to much wetter than normal over much of the state as an active monsoon pattern dominated, particularly the second half of the month. Widespread thunderstorm rainfall chiseled away at long-term drought and the percentage of New Mexico in severe drought or worse decreased nearly 40 percent by month's end.  Areas of exceptional drought in far northeast New Mexico were eliminated.  Month-to-date rainfall totaled more than twice normal in several New Mexico locations, including Albuquerque (3.34 inches, or 242 percent of normal) that experienced its 7th wettest July on record. Still, New Mexico���s rangeland was rated 65% very poor to poor on July 27, slightly worse than the late-July, 5-year average of 62%. In addition, subsoil moisture was rated very short to short.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system that produced widespread strong winds across the region on the 7th moved over central New Mexico on the 8th and produced scattered showers with hail and gusty winds.  Several locations reported graupel and small hail coating the ground, including Gallup, Taos, Albuquerque, and Manzano.  While most reports were of small hail, one area around Manzano experienced penny size hail for a few minutes.

An upper level disturbance moved over the region, and the atmosphere was very unstable with a lot of moisture.  There was an outflow/frontal boundary across the area which provided a focus for thunderstorms.  An upper level jet was near the area which enhanced lift.  Winds were changing direction and increasing with height which allowed for more rotation in the air.  This allowed storms to become severe and increased the potential for tornadoes to develop.

A dryline was present across Lea County and the Upper Trans Pecos with good moisture to the east of this line.  The atmosphere was very unstable, and there was a surface trough across the higher terrain of southeast New Mexico and West Texas.  These conditions contributed to flash flooding across southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain moved across Lea County and produced flash flooding on Highway 62/180 west of Hobbs.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably potent upper level storm system moving southeast across the central Rockies pushed into the Four Corners region on the 11th.  A deep area of surface low pressure over the eastern plains tapped strong winds aloft with this storm system and produced areas of strong winds and some blowing dust over south central New Mexico. The strongest winds occurred within the area from Carrizozo to Ruidoso and Clines Corners where gusts between 58 and 67 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sandia tram 61 mph around noon.  Many public reports including Tijeras and Chilili of strong winds in the 50 to 55 mph range throughout the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The edge of an upper ridge was over southeast New Mexico and West Texas as an upper level disturbance moved over the area.  Several outflow boundaries were across the area which provided a focus for thunderstorms to develop.  There was good low level moisture present and the atmosphere was unstable.  These conditions resulted in high wind gusts and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Lea County and produced a 62 mph wind gust.  There was blowing dust as a result of this wind.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was over southern Arizona with upper level disturbances moving over New Mexico and the Big Bend region.  Residual outflow boundaries from thunderstorms the previous day were present across the area helping to provide additional lift.  There was a lot of moisture in the air so this aided in heavy rainfall.  The atmosphere became very unstable with an increase in heating, especially in the higher terrain.  This allowed for severe thunderstorms to develop across West Texas and southeast New Mexico with hail, flash flooding, and a tornado.

An upper level disturbance moved over the region, and the atmosphere was very unstable with a lot of moisture.  There was an outflow/frontal boundary across the area which provided a focus for thunderstorms.  An upper level jet was near the area which enhanced lift.  Winds were changing direction and increasing with height which allowed for more rotation in the air.  This allowed storms to become severe and increased the potential for tornadoes to develop.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pedernal estimated peak wind gust to 60 mph with some light damage to tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a very cold upper level storm system for mid May and a powerful cold front within central New Mexico produced widespread light snowfall in many areas along and north of Interstate 40.  Heavy snowfall occurred in the Sangre de Cristo Mountains where the strongest upslope flow and upper level forcing coincided.  Generally 8 to 11 inches of snow was reported in the higher terrain.  A peak wind gust of 63 mph was reported within the Upper Tularosa Valley as gap winds set up over the region.  Roadways become slick and icy across portions of northern New Mexico.  Highway 68 was closed south of Taos due to inclement weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Phillips Hill reported numerous gusts over 58 mph with a peak at 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated mid level moisture allowed for isolated showers and thunderstorms to develop across portions of the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and drift eastward into the evening. One of these cells that was right along the Colorado New Mexico border near Folsom produced quarter size hail before quickly dissipating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail covering the ground just north of Folsom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The slow-moving upper level storm responsible for severe weather over New Mexico on the 22nd continued to impact the area on the 23rd.  Showers and thunderstorms developed over the high plains and moved east as a squall line through the early morning hours on the 24th.  Several reports of pea to quarter size hail, strong damaging winds, and flooding were received across eastern New Mexico. A motorcyclist was struck by lightning and killed near Cimarron.  The Roswell Airpark set an all-time record 24-hour rainfall amount of 4.44 inches dating back to 1949. Widespread flooding was reported particularly on the east side of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail accumulated 4 to 5 inches along Interstate 40 near Wagon Wheel causing significant impact to travelers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was over Arizona/New Mexico slowly moving eastward with upper level disturbances associated with this low moving over southeast New Mexico and West Texas.  The atmosphere was very moist and unstable and there were southeast upslope winds leading to an increase in lift.  There was an outflow boundary near the Pecos River which provided a concentrated source of lift for thunderstorms to develop.  Recent rainfall had moistened the soil enough to increase the threat of flash flooding.  These conditions resulted in severe thunderstorms with hail, strong winds, and flash flooding across West Texas and southeast New Mexico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: July 2014 was wetter to much wetter than normal over much of the state as an active monsoon pattern dominated, particularly the second half of the month. Widespread thunderstorm rainfall chiseled away at long-term drought and the percentage of New Mexico in severe drought or worse decreased nearly 40 percent by month's end.  Areas of exceptional drought in far northeast New Mexico were eliminated.  Month-to-date rainfall totaled more than twice normal in several New Mexico locations, including Albuquerque (3.34 inches, or 242 percent of normal) that experienced its 7th wettest July on record. Still, New Mexico���s rangeland was rated 65% very poor to poor on July 27, slightly worse than the late-July, 5-year average of 62%. In addition, subsoil moisture was rated very short to short.

An upper low was located along the Arizona/New Mexico border progressing slowly eastward into the northern Texas Panhandle with associated upper-level disturbances rotating around the base of the upper trough.  Outflow boundaries from convection the previous day were the focus for thunderstorm development across the Trans Pecos and Permian Basin along with strong heating in the higher terrain.  Moist and unstable atmospheric conditions were conducive for potential flooding and supercell development.  Also, winds were changing direction and speed with increasing elevation in the atmosphere to support tornado development.

A cold front had moved into the area and was stalled along the higher terrain which provided a focus for thunderstorms.  An upper level disturbance had moved near the region increasing lift.  There was an abundance of moisture across the area and plenty of daytime heating which helped to increase instability.  As a result of these conditions, severe thunderstorms moved across southeast New Mexico and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Eddy County and produced a 68 mph wind gust in Artesia.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was over Arizona/New Mexico slowly moving eastward with upper level disturbances associated with this low moving over southeast New Mexico and West Texas.  The atmosphere was very moist and unstable and there were southeast upslope winds leading to an increase in lift.  There was an outflow boundary near the Pecos River which provided a concentrated source of lift for thunderstorms to develop.  Recent rainfall had moistened the soil enough to increase the threat of flash flooding.  These conditions resulted in severe thunderstorms with hail, strong winds, and flash flooding across West Texas and southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Eddy County and produced flash flooding near Carlsbad.  There was a high water rescue at the intersection of County Road 408 and Hidalgo Road about eight miles south southwest of Carlsbad.  The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

A potent spring storm system crossed New Mexico and battered much of the area with strong winds, blowing dust, and mainly dry showers.  A cold frontal boundary slammed through western New Mexico during the afternoon hours and produced many areas of strong winds right along and immediately behind the front.  Just enough moisture and instability was in place for central and eastern New Mexico to produce scattered virga showers ahead of the front.  These showers exacerbated the windy conditions and pulled stronger winds aloft to the surface.  A recreation center in Las Vegas suffered roof damage as a 67 mph wind gust slammed the area.  Many other locales also reported wind gusts between 58 and 67 mph throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Phillips Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough was moving through the region with sufficient moisture still in place from previous days. The northwest flow aloft and weak south to southwest winds at the surface provided sufficient shear for severe thunderstorms with heavy rain to develop across Otero County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Otero County Sheriff reported 2 feet of water flowing over some roads in Orogrande. The Orogrande Cooperative Observer reported 2.96 inches of rain and the SAMS site 5 miles west-northwest of Orogrande recorded 2.03 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief break from heavy rainfall across the state, yet another strong and very moist back door frontal boundary sagged southwest into New Mexico.  The upper level high pressure center was generally over far southeast New Mexico allowing for moist southerly flow in the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere to impact the area as well.  Slow-moving thunderstorms produced torrential rainfall across many areas during the early to mid afternoon hours, including Santa Fe, Rio Rancho, and Albuquerque. The heaviest rainfall occurred over northeast New Mexico during the evening and overnight hours.  Raton Crews Airport picked up a record 3.99 inches of rainfall with an impressive 4.91 inches at Mosquero.  Incredibly, no reports of damage or flash flooding were received across northeastern New Mexico. However, rises were noted on several area creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall produced 4.38 inches at Mosquero.  This was the 2nd highest 24-hour precipitation total reported at Mosquero dating back to 1926.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent back door cold front surged westward late on June 30th and introduced the first significant moisture surge of the 2014 monsoon season.  The upper level high was centered over northwestern New Mexico and allowed storms to slide slowly east southeast off the Continental Divide into nearby valley locations.  A severe storm near Cubero produced penny sized hail and heavy rainfall during the late afternoon hours.  Another severe thunderstorm moved over Taos very late in the evening and produced nickel sized hail around the Blueberry Hill area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail and heavy rainfall.  Minor flooding in area arroyos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active and persistent pattern maintained a heightened threat for flash flooding across much of the state. An upper level high was centered over portions of northern New Mexico and southern Colorado. A disturbance rotating around the upper high slowly shifted from west central to north central areas throughout the overnight hours. Weak steering flow aloft combined with a very moist airmass in place fueled slow-moving thunderstorms that produced very heavy rainfall and flash flooding. Showers and thunderstorms began developing during the late morning hours on the 28th over the Southwest Mountains, the Lower Rio Grande Valley, the West Central Plateau and the Northern Mountains. Coverage expanded and intensified into the afternoon hours with small, heavy rain-producing cells. Activity slowly began to decrease into the evening hours. However, by 3am, a large swath of light to moderate rain with embedded heavy precipitation had developed across the central highlands. Thunderstorms began training over the area from Santa Rosa eastward to near Newkirk. This swath of precipitation slowly moved eastward throughout the early morning hours finally subsiding by 11am the 29th. Due to ground already saturated from the previous days rainfall, significant flooding occurred across portions of Guadalupe and De Baca counties.  In particular, major flash flooding was reported within the Alamogordo Creek drainage.  River flooding was also reported along the Pecos River near Puerto de Luna.  Radar estimated storm total accumulations ranged from 7 to 13 inches in this area. These torrential rains washed out the supports underneath train tracks near Santa Rosa leaving the track suspended in air. Numerous other reports of flooding including asphalt up-ended and roads closed due to flowing water occurred in east central areas. ||Converging outflow boundaries from storms in Western New Mexico created enough rotation to produce small rope like funnel clouds as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 3.10 inches of rainfall overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper level pattern continued to be dominated by broad high pressure over the state. An upper level disturbance tracked eastward across central and northern New Mexico triggering showers and thunderstorms over much of the area. Moisture remained abundant with precipitable water vales nearly 150% of normal. Showers and thunderstorms developed during the late morning hours across central portions of the state. These storms slowly moved eastward throughout the afternoon. During the late afternoon hours, a thunderstorm developed in far northwest Bernalillo County and intensified as it moved east southeast into the Albuquerque South Valley.  This storm dropped over one inch of rainfall in less than 45 minutes causing significant flooding in Albuquerque's South Valley. Another isolated strong thunderstorm near Bernal in San Miguel county produced a well-defined funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media relayed a photo of a funnel cloud near Bernal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While the overall coverage of showers and thunderstorms began to diminish on the 17th, the storms that did manage to develop were strong to severe.  A storm that impacted the area around Black Lake produced penny sized hail that covered the ground.  Another persistent area of heavy rainfall from strong storms produced significant flooding around La Madera.  A foot bridge was washed out near a local farm and washed several miles downstream on the Rio Tusas.  Another strong storm blew up in the Santa Fe foothills and produced penny sized hail.  The strongest storm of the day developed around Las Vegas during the very late afternoon hours and moved south for several hours through the high plains over eastern New Mexico before dissipating along the Capitan Mountains.  Residents are Encino reported strong rotation in this thunderstorm with many suspected reports of funnel clouds.  Radar data suggested hail as large as baseballs possible with this storm.  A rural mesonet site recorded a peak wind gust to near 80 mph as the storm blasted through.  One final round of funnel clouds and quarter size hail was reported east of Encinoso as the storm was dissipating.

This was the second day in a mutli-day significant severe weather outbreak across eastern New Mexico. A back door cold front moved into the northeast and east central portions of the state throughout the morning then nudged up against the central mountain chain by the afternoon hours. Deep moisture and instability aided in the development of supercell thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours along the east slopes of the central mountain chain that moved into the eastern plains. Large, damaging hail, strong winds, heavy downpours, and tornadoes were reported. Hail ranging from 2 to 4 inches in diameter caused significant damage to windows and vehicles as well as livestock and homes around Corona and Clovis.  A tornado was also reported in Corona with only damage to open rangeland.  Another thunderstorm that moved through Ranchvale and Clovis produced widespread wind damage.

A moist backdoor cold front pushed southwest across the eastern plains and through the central mountains. Strong instability and sufficient low level shear set the stage for a significant round of severe weather across eastern New Mexico. Storms began to form during the early afternoon hours with many cells becoming severe by midday. Several storms produced large hail, strong winds, and a few tornadoes.  A powerful storm near Maxwell produced hen egg size hail and significant damage to cars and windows. A supercell thunderstorm that developed near Ranchvale and progressed across the north side of Clovis produced a tornado in Ranchvale and widespread wind and hail damage in Clovis. An impressive 79mph wind gust was reported at Cannon Air Force Base. The NMSU Agricultural Science Center suffered major damage to crops, buildings, and vehicles from hail. Thunderstorms continued in the northeast portion of the state into the following morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CWOP station northwest of Sumner Lake recorded a 59mph thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms with strong winds, tornadoes, large hail, and heavy rainfall impacted New Mexico on this third in a series of significant severe weather days.  Thunderstorms developed along the central mountain chain early in the afternoon then moved slowly east southeast across the plains.  Several small short-lived tornadoes developed along the high plains, in particular around Maxwell, Wagon Mound, Valmora, Springer, and Encinoso.  The Encinoso tornado destroyed 2 homes and severely damaged 2 others.  Many reports of quarter to baseball size hail were also reported with these storms.  The largest hail reported was around 3 inches in diameter east of Interstate 25 near Valmora.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most areas received near to above average precipitation during the month of August. The month started out with a large area in Extreme(D3) drought, but by the end of the month, just a small area in Hidalgo and Grant Counties had some areas of Extreme(D3) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The beginning of the month started with Extreme(D3) drought everywhere except the southeast where Severe(D2) drought was observed. By the end of the month, the northwestern portion of the zone was put into Severe(D2) drought with no change elsewhere. The Antelope Wells Cooperative Observer reported 2.22 inches of rain which was 0.35 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most areas received near to above average precipitation during the month of August. The month started out with a large area in Extreme(D3) drought, but by the end of the month, just a small area in Hidalgo and Grant Counties had some areas of Extreme(D3) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month started with Extreme(D3) drought for all areas except the far north where it was Severe(D2). By the end of the month, the entire zone was in Severe(D2) drought. The Deming Airport reported 1.01 inches of rain which was .58 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most areas received near to above average precipitation during the month of August. The month started out with a large area in Extreme(D3) drought, but by the end of the month, just a small area in Hidalgo and Grant Counties had some areas of Extreme(D3) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southern half of the zone started the month in Severe(D2) drought and Moderate(D1) drought north. Only the far southern portions of the zone remained in Severe(D2) drought at the end of the month with the remainder of the areas in Abnormally Dry(D0) or Moderate(D1) drought.  The Truth or Consequences Airport received 1.85 inches of precipitation which was 0.24 inches above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was a cold front near the Pecos River on the eleventh which provided a source of convergence and lift.  This front shifted north over the central and northern Permian Basin as well as southeast New Mexico that night.  The atmosphere was very moist and unstable.  There were weak winds aloft which allowed for slower moving storms and rain contributing to flash flooding.  Another cold front moved through the area on the twelfth.  Upper level disturbances were also over the region which allowed for more atmospheric lift.  These conditions combined to produce flash flooding across southeast New Mexico and West Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Eddy County and produced flash flooding southwest of Carlsbad.  Dark Canyon Road and County Road 408 were closed due to an estimated five feet of water over the road at Juniper Draw.  The amount of damage is unknown so the cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just after midnight on the morning of the 16th, a line of strong storms crossed south eastward across Interstate 40 near Santa Rosa. This storm produced two lightning strikes that hit a rest area and gas station causing power failures. ||The greatest amount of atmospheric moisture available to date for the summer of 2014 was in place across much of central New Mexico.  Light northwest steering flow aloft beneath an upper level high pressure center building over southwestern New Mexico allowed for locally heavy rainfall.  An area of thunderstorms developed over the southern slopes of the Jemez Mountains forced development farther south into the Albuquerque Metro Area.  These storms developed from northwest to southeast and continually trained over the center of Albuquerque for nearly an hour.  The heaviest rainfall impacted the area from near Montano and Coors southeast across the UNM area, the Albuquerque Sunport, the area around Juan Tabo and Copper, and Mesa del Sol. Significant standing water was reported along Coors Boulevard. Strong flows created impassible conditions along Washington Avenue between Indian School and Constitution.  A frightening YouTube video was posted of the Embudo Canyon flood channel with water battering water beneath of overpass along Interstate 40.  The water was only about 5 feet away from pouring out onto Interstate 40. The most significant flooding occurred around Mesa del Sol where several homes took in water several inches deep.  Many yards in the area reported around 6 inches of standing water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intersection closed at Indian School and Carlisle likely due to combination of heavy rainfall over an existing water main excavation.
