EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active and persistent pattern maintained a heightened threat for flash flooding across much of the state. An upper level high was centered over portions of northern New Mexico and southern Colorado. A disturbance rotating around the upper high slowly shifted from west central to north central areas throughout the overnight hours. Weak steering flow aloft combined with a very moist airmass in place fueled slow-moving thunderstorms that produced very heavy rainfall and flash flooding. Showers and thunderstorms began developing during the late morning hours on the 28th over the Southwest Mountains, the Lower Rio Grande Valley, the West Central Plateau and the Northern Mountains. Coverage expanded and intensified into the afternoon hours with small, heavy rain-producing cells. Activity slowly began to decrease into the evening hours. However, by 3am, a large swath of light to moderate rain with embedded heavy precipitation had developed across the central highlands. Thunderstorms began training over the area from Santa Rosa eastward to near Newkirk. This swath of precipitation slowly moved eastward throughout the early morning hours finally subsiding by 11am the 29th. Due to ground already saturated from the previous days rainfall, significant flooding occurred across portions of Guadalupe and De Baca counties.  In particular, major flash flooding was reported within the Alamogordo Creek drainage.  River flooding was also reported along the Pecos River near Puerto de Luna.  Radar estimated storm total accumulations ranged from 7 to 13 inches in this area. These torrential rains washed out the supports underneath train tracks near Santa Rosa leaving the track suspended in air. Numerous other reports of flooding including asphalt up-ended and roads closed due to flowing water occurred in east central areas. ||Converging outflow boundaries from storms in Western New Mexico created enough rotation to produce small rope like funnel clouds as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One mile north of House reported 4.25 inches of rainfall and minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most areas received near to above average precipitation during the month of August. The month started out with a large area in Extreme(D3) drought, but by the end of the month, just a small area in Hidalgo and Grant Counties had some areas of Extreme(D3) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Besides the northwest corner of the zone which was in Moderate(D1) drought, the remainder of the zone started August in Severe(D2) drought. On August 5th, the southern half of the zone remained in Severe(D2) drought which gradually improved northward to Abnormally Dry(D0) drought. The White Sands Missile Range Cooperative Observer reported 2.31 inches of rain which was 0.56 inches above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most areas received near to above average precipitation during the month of August. The month started out with a large area in Extreme(D3) drought, but by the end of the month, just a small area in Hidalgo and Grant Counties had some areas of Extreme(D3) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month started with Extreme(D3) drought far west and Severe(D2) drought the remainder of the area. On August 5th, all areas were put into Severe(D2) drought except the far south which improved to Moderate(D1) drought. The Cooperative Observer at Orogrande reported 2.90 inches of precipitation which was 0.92 inches above normal.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month of August started with Extreme(D3) drought northeast and Severe(D2) drought for the remainder of the zone. By the end of the month conditions improved with all areas in Severe(D2) drought except the far south where it was in Moderate(D1) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist backdoor cold front pushed southwest across the eastern plains and through the central mountains. Strong instability and sufficient low level shear set the stage for a significant round of severe weather across eastern New Mexico. Storms began to form during the early afternoon hours with many cells becoming severe by midday. Several storms produced large hail, strong winds, and a few tornadoes.  A powerful storm near Maxwell produced hen egg size hail and significant damage to cars and windows. A supercell thunderstorm that developed near Ranchvale and progressed across the north side of Clovis produced a tornado in Ranchvale and widespread wind and hail damage in Clovis. An impressive 79mph wind gust was reported at Cannon Air Force Base. The NMSU Agricultural Science Center suffered major damage to crops, buildings, and vehicles from hail. Thunderstorms continued in the northeast portion of the state into the following morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee storm chasing observed 2 inch diameter hail (hen egg size).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the second day in a mutli-day significant severe weather outbreak across eastern New Mexico. A back door cold front moved into the northeast and east central portions of the state throughout the morning then nudged up against the central mountain chain by the afternoon hours. Deep moisture and instability aided in the development of supercell thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours along the east slopes of the central mountain chain that moved into the eastern plains. Large, damaging hail, strong winds, heavy downpours, and tornadoes were reported. Hail ranging from 2 to 4 inches in diameter caused significant damage to windows and vehicles as well as livestock and homes around Corona and Clovis.  A tornado was also reported in Corona with only damage to open rangeland.  Another thunderstorm that moved through Ranchvale and Clovis produced widespread wind damage.

Numerous thunderstorms with strong winds, tornadoes, large hail, and heavy rainfall impacted New Mexico on this third in a series of significant severe weather days.  Thunderstorms developed along the central mountain chain early in the afternoon then moved slowly east southeast across the plains.  Several small short-lived tornadoes developed along the high plains, in particular around Maxwell, Wagon Mound, Valmora, Springer, and Encinoso.  The Encinoso tornado destroyed 2 homes and severely damaged 2 others.  Many reports of quarter to baseball size hail were also reported with these storms.  The largest hail reported was around 3 inches in diameter east of Interstate 25 near Valmora.

A moisture rich atmosphere set up across New Mexico behind the passage of a potent cold front the day before.  Strong thunderstorms developed by late afternoon over the northern high terrain.  Light northeast steering flow forced these storms southwest into the Upper Rio Grande Valley and the Lower Chama River Valley by late in the evening.  Torrential rainfall within a line of slow-moving storms impacted the area from Abiquiu southeast to El Rancho, Pojoaque, and Nambe. Heavy rain and resultant runoff from one particularly strong storm washed several mobile homes off their foundations at the Pojoaque Terrace Mobile Homes Estates.  A broken gas line forced the evacuation of the entire community.  A total of 23 homes were damaged.  Another heavy storm washed out a culvert and associated roadway northeast of Abiquiu.  An even heavier downpour around La Puebla and the Estrella del Norte winery prompted a swift water rescue within an arroyo where 3 people were trapped. Officials also responded to a water rescue around El Rancho.  These heavy thunderstorms were followed by several hours of moderate rainfall which continued arroyos running very high through the following morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A water rescue occurred in El Rancho from flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief break from heavy rainfall across the state, yet another strong and very moist back door frontal boundary sagged southwest into New Mexico.  The upper level high pressure center was generally over far southeast New Mexico allowing for moist southerly flow in the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere to impact the area as well.  Slow-moving thunderstorms produced torrential rainfall across many areas during the early to mid afternoon hours, including Santa Fe, Rio Rancho, and Albuquerque. The heaviest rainfall occurred over northeast New Mexico during the evening and overnight hours.  Raton Crews Airport picked up a record 3.99 inches of rainfall with an impressive 4.91 inches at Mosquero.  Incredibly, no reports of damage or flash flooding were received across northeastern New Mexico. However, rises were noted on several area creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photo passed along from KOAT showed a wash out along an arroyo in Santa Fe.  The berm estimated to be 4 or 5 feet high was completely gone and remnant water was still flowing through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief break from heavy rainfall across the state, yet another strong and very moist back door frontal boundary sagged southwest into New Mexico. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media reported flooding of the Vietnam Veterans Memorial Park. Softball  players showed up to play only to find the softball fields completely underwater. Standing water was estimated at least 4 feet deep in areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread slow-moving heavy thunderstorms that moved eastward across the eastern plains very early on the morning of the 2nd produced torrential rainfall from De Baca County eastward into Curry and Roosevelt counties.  Two to 4 inches of rain were reported across much of this area, including as much as 4.5 inches in Clovis.  Many roads around Fort Sumner became impassable due to high water.  Clovis experienced dangerous flash flooding with significant street flooding.  A car along a section of 14th street was submerged in 8 feet of water.  Standing water and impacts to roadways across the region continued throughout the day, well after storms had exited the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The City of Clovis has 3 detention ponds that are located in the upper portions of 3 small north-south oriented watersheds on the north side of the city. During Wednesday's event, the heaviest rain was anchored over and just upstream of the 3 ponds and, as a result, they quickly filled and began to spill. A lifetime resident of Clovis said it was the first time in at least 10 years that all three ponds filled, and it was the first time in recent memory that the Dennis Chavez pond spilled. The police and fire departments received about 120 calls in one hour between 6:45am and 7:45 of stalled vehicles and at least 3 vehicles that were completely submerged. Police responded to 8 water rescues from vehicles. One vehicle along 14th street was submerged in 8 feet of water. Fortunately, no injuries or major damage.  The owner of Enchanted Spirits Studios noted that up to 1 inch of water entered the business causing some carpet damage.  Fire Station #6 and one house near Dennis Chavez park (pond) were the only structures that had damage from high water and the damage estimate there was $250,000. Flooding was also reported around Green Acres Park.  Water receded very slowly through the day well after rainfall had ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful back door cold front surged southwest across the eastern plains during the afternoon of the 14th then plowed west through the Rio Grande Valley.  The combination of deep moisture and strong lift associated with the front along with weak steering flows beneath the upper level high produced widespread heavy rainfall. A couple severe thunderstorms erupted over the area given the presence of strong vertical wind shear.  The most severe flooding occurred well after sunset when training thunderstorms around the Espanola area produced prolific rainfall amounts.  A CoCoRaHS observer to the northwest of Espanola reported an impressive 4.88 inches of rainfall from this storm.  Major flash flooding was observed around Espanola.  A total of 8 homes were damaged by flood waters and numerous vehicles were abandoned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall produced significant flooding in Espanola during the overnight on the 14-15th.  The NM Emergency Operations Center and local broadcast media reported at least 8 homes suffered flood damage with numerous vehicles abandoned and flooded. At least 4 residents reported 12 to 18 inches of standing water on their properties.  North Railroad Ave and Hunter were flooded forcing city officials to run pumps to remove water. East Laguna Street around Santa Claran Casino was flooded.  Santa Clara Pueblo was also flooded during this event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Leaves shredded off Aspen trees around Chama.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms with heavy rainfall developed along the Continental Divide early on the 29th and pushed slowly east toward the Rio Grande Valley through the evening hours.  Several strong cells produced hail over portions of the area. Nickel size hail and heavy rain impacted Arroyo Seco.  Hail was also reported near Las Vegas and Moriarty.  A powerful storm near Abiquiu produced torrential rainfall, flooding, and damaging hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least two roads were washed out near Beaverhead from heavy rainfall.  Damage estimated for repairs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep surge of low level moisture westward into central and eastern New Mexico interacted with an approaching upper level storm system from the Great Basin to produce another round of severe weather for the eastern plains.  Thunderstorms developed along a line over Socorro, Torrance, and Lincoln counties then advanced eastward into a bow echo over much of the plains.  The main impact from these storms was high winds.  Areas from Tucumcari south to Clovis reported peak winds between 60 and 70 mph.  No damage was reported.  Heavy rain and frequent lightning strikes accompanied these storms across the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cannon Air Force Base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving and unseasonably deep upper level trough positioned over the Great Basin combined with a surface lee trough over eastern New Mexico to produce widespread windy conditions.  Winds increased by late morning over the entire state with most areas experiencing peak winds around 45 to 50 mph. A couple of the more wind prone locations such as Gallup and Clines Corners reported peak winds near 60 mph.  No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sierra Blanca Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough that approached from the Great Basin combined with sufficient moisture over portions of eastern New Mexico to produce isolated severe thunderstorms.  The strongest storms developed during the early evening hours and pushed across a few areas from near Fort Sumner north to Folsom.  The largest hail reported was up to quarter size.  No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sumner Lake.

The Encinoso tornado destroyed 2 homes and severely damaged 2 others.  Many reports of quarter to baseball size hail were also reported with these storms.  The largest hail reported was around 3 inches in diameter east of Interstate 25 near Valmora.

A back door cold front moved into the region with east winds bringing in deep moisture up to about the Rio Grande Valley. Upper level flow was out of the northwest providing sufficient shear for some severe thunderstorms to develop and some isolated flash flooding. Wind gusts up to 66 mph and hail up to an inch and a half in diameter were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: May have been outflow from storms well east of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture rich atmosphere set up across New Mexico behind the passage of a potent cold front the day before.  Strong thunderstorms developed by late afternoon over the northern high terrain.  Light northeast steering flow forced these storms southwest into the Upper Rio Grande Valley and the Lower Chama River Valley by late in the evening.  Torrential rainfall within a line of slow-moving storms impacted the area from Abiquiu southeast to El Rancho, Pojoaque, and Nambe. Heavy rain and resultant runoff from one particularly strong storm washed several mobile homes off their foundations at the Pojoaque Terrace Mobile Homes Estates.  A broken gas line forced the evacuation of the entire community.  A total of 23 homes were damaged.  Another heavy storm washed out a culvert and associated roadway northeast of Abiquiu.  An even heavier downpour around La Puebla and the Estrella del Norte winery prompted a swift water rescue within an arroyo where 3 people were trapped. Officials also responded to a water rescue around El Rancho.  These heavy thunderstorms were followed by several hours of moderate rainfall which continued arroyos running very high through the following morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty minutes of intense rainfall followed by 4 hours of moderate rainfall caused wash out of County Road 155.  A culvert beneath the road could not hold all the water and breached the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active and persistent pattern maintained a heightened threat for flash flooding across much of the state. An upper level high was centered over portions of northern New Mexico and southern Colorado. A disturbance rotating around the upper high slowly shifted from west central to north central areas throughout the overnight hours. Weak steering flow aloft combined with a very moist airmass in place fueled slow-moving thunderstorms that produced very heavy rainfall and flash flooding. Showers and thunderstorms began developing during the late morning hours on the 28th over the Southwest Mountains, the Lower Rio Grande Valley, the West Central Plateau and the Northern Mountains. Coverage expanded and intensified into the afternoon hours with small, heavy rain-producing cells. Activity slowly began to decrease into the evening hours. However, by 3am, a large swath of light to moderate rain with embedded heavy precipitation had developed across the central highlands. Thunderstorms began training over the area from Santa Rosa eastward to near Newkirk. This swath of precipitation slowly moved eastward throughout the early morning hours finally subsiding by 11am the 29th. Due to ground already saturated from the previous days rainfall, significant flooding occurred across portions of Guadalupe and De Baca counties.  In particular, major flash flooding was reported within the Alamogordo Creek drainage.  River flooding was also reported along the Pecos River near Puerto de Luna.  Radar estimated storm total accumulations ranged from 7 to 13 inches in this area. These torrential rains washed out the supports underneath train tracks near Santa Rosa leaving the track suspended in air. Numerous other reports of flooding including asphalt up-ended and roads closed due to flowing water occurred in east central areas. ||Converging outflow boundaries from storms in Western New Mexico created enough rotation to produce small rope like funnel clouds as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pecos River at Puerto de Luna reached 11.01 feet at 5pm pm on the 29th.  This was just barely over flood stage at this point.  Farmers and locals in the area said the areal flooding in parts of the area around the Pecos was worse than the previous flooding from September 2013.  The county was expected to assess the damage to minor farming structures along the river when the water receeded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread slow-moving heavy thunderstorms that moved eastward across the eastern plains very early on the morning of the 2nd produced torrential rainfall from De Baca County eastward into Curry and Roosevelt counties.  Two to 4 inches of rain were reported across much of this area, including as much as 4.5 inches in Clovis.  Many roads around Fort Sumner became impassable due to high water.  Clovis experienced dangerous flash flooding with significant street flooding.  A car along a section of 14th street was submerged in 8 feet of water.  Standing water and impacts to roadways across the region continued throughout the day, well after storms had exited the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall observed upstream of Yeso Creek produced a significant rise near Highway 20 south of Fort Sumner.  The KFDX storm total rainfall accumulation product indicated 8+ inches over and just upstream of this location, and there was definitely evidence of high water but no major flooding found.  The NM DOT reported around 3 inches of water over the road along Yeso Creek east of Highway 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While the overall coverage of showers and thunderstorms began to diminish on the 17th, the storms that did manage to develop were strong to severe.  A storm that impacted the area around Black Lake produced penny sized hail that covered the ground.  Another persistent area of heavy rainfall from strong storms produced significant flooding around La Madera.  A foot bridge was washed out near a local farm and washed several miles downstream on the Rio Tusas.  Another strong storm blew up in the Santa Fe foothills and produced penny sized hail.  The strongest storm of the day developed around Las Vegas during the very late afternoon hours and moved south for several hours through the high plains over eastern New Mexico before dissipating along the Capitan Mountains.  Residents are Encino reported strong rotation in this thunderstorm with many suspected reports of funnel clouds.  Radar data suggested hail as large as baseballs possible with this storm.  A rural mesonet site recorded a peak wind gust to near 80 mph as the storm blasted through.  One final round of funnel clouds and quarter size hail was reported east of Encinoso as the storm was dissipating.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported 3 people were rescued from 2 vehicles in an arroyo in La Puebla.  Local broadcast media reported just down the road that a clogged culvert around the Estrella del Norte Vineyard created a flood of water, mud, and debris through the winery.  No major damage was reported to structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough that approached from the Great Basin combined with sufficient moisture over portions of eastern New Mexico to produce isolated severe thunderstorms.  The strongest storms developed during the early evening hours and pushed across a few areas from near Fort Sumner north to Folsom.  The largest hail reported was up to quarter size.  No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail up to 2 inches deep at State Road 551 and 456 to the northeast of Folsom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of strong to severe thunderstorms developed over eastern New Mexico by late in the day on the 23rd as a late season upper level strong approached from the west and interacted with deep moisture and instability over the plains.  The strongest storms produced mainly large hail and heavy rainfall. A second round of strong to severe thunderstorms redeveloped over the region after the first round of storms earlier in the evening.  These storms continued to produce hail and heavy rainfall.  Quarter size hail was reported around Raton.  Rainfall amounts between 1 and 2 inches were reported over much of Union County.  No damage or widespread flooding was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maxwell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist backdoor cold front pushed southwest across the eastern plains and through the central mountains. Strong instability and sufficient low level shear set the stage for a significant round of severe weather across eastern New Mexico. Storms began to form during the early afternoon hours with many cells becoming severe by midday. Several storms produced large hail, strong winds, and a few tornadoes.  A powerful storm near Maxwell produced hen egg size hail and significant damage to cars and windows. A supercell thunderstorm that developed near Ranchvale and progressed across the north side of Clovis produced a tornado in Ranchvale and widespread wind and hail damage in Clovis. An impressive 79mph wind gust was reported at Cannon Air Force Base. The NMSU Agricultural Science Center suffered major damage to crops, buildings, and vehicles from hail. Thunderstorms continued in the northeast portion of the state into the following morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer noted significant swirling dust and 1.25 inch hail.

This was the second day in a mutli-day significant severe weather outbreak across eastern New Mexico. A back door cold front moved into the northeast and east central portions of the state throughout the morning then nudged up against the central mountain chain by the afternoon hours. Deep moisture and instability aided in the development of supercell thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours along the east slopes of the central mountain chain that moved into the eastern plains. Large, damaging hail, strong winds, heavy downpours, and tornadoes were reported. Hail ranging from 2 to 4 inches in diameter caused significant damage to windows and vehicles as well as livestock and homes around Corona and Clovis.  A tornado was also reported in Corona with only damage to open rangeland.  Another thunderstorm that moved through Ranchvale and Clovis produced widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter observed funnel cloud near Sedan with significant rotation in thunderstorm structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms with strong winds, tornadoes, large hail, and heavy rainfall impacted New Mexico on this third in a series of significant severe weather days.  Thunderstorms developed along the central mountain chain early in the afternoon then moved slowly east southeast across the plains.  Several small short-lived tornadoes developed along the high plains, in particular around Maxwell, Wagon Mound, Valmora, Springer, and Encinoso.  The Encinoso tornado destroyed 2 homes and severely damaged 2 others.  Many reports of quarter to baseball size hail were also reported with these storms.  The largest hail reported was around 3 inches in diameter east of Interstate 25 near Valmora.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorm spawned a brief tornado that crossed Interstate 25 near mile marker 375.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough was moving through the region with sufficient moisture still in place from previous days. The northwest flow aloft and weak south to southwest winds at the surface provided sufficient shear for severe thunderstorms with heavy rain to develop across Otero County.

A weak upper level trough was moving through the region with sufficient moisture still in place from previous days. The northwest flow aloft and weak south to southwest winds at the surface provided sufficient shear for severe thunderstorms with heavy rain to develop across Otero County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A SAMS tower 5 miles west-northwest of Orogrande recorded a peak gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just after midnight on the morning of the 16th, a line of strong storms crossed south eastward across Interstate 40 near Santa Rosa. This storm produced two lightning strikes that hit a rest area and gas station causing power failures. ||The greatest amount of atmospheric moisture available to date for the summer of 2014 was in place across much of central New Mexico.  Light northwest steering flow aloft beneath an upper level high pressure center building over southwestern New Mexico allowed for locally heavy rainfall.  An area of thunderstorms developed over the southern slopes of the Jemez Mountains forced development farther south into the Albuquerque Metro Area.  These storms developed from northwest to southeast and continually trained over the center of Albuquerque for nearly an hour.  The heaviest rainfall impacted the area from near Montano and Coors southeast across the UNM area, the Albuquerque Sunport, the area around Juan Tabo and Copper, and Mesa del Sol. Significant standing water was reported along Coors Boulevard. Strong flows created impassible conditions along Washington Avenue between Indian School and Constitution.  A frightening YouTube video was posted of the Embudo Canyon flood channel with water battering water beneath of overpass along Interstate 40.  The water was only about 5 feet away from pouring out onto Interstate 40. The most significant flooding occurred around Mesa del Sol where several homes took in water several inches deep.  Many yards in the area reported around 6 inches of standing water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Washington impassible near Arturo Park due to strong flow over roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief break from heavy rainfall across the state, yet another strong and very moist back door frontal boundary sagged southwest into New Mexico.  The upper level high pressure center was generally over far southeast New Mexico allowing for moist southerly flow in the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere to impact the area as well.  Slow-moving thunderstorms produced torrential rainfall across many areas during the early to mid afternoon hours, including Santa Fe, Rio Rancho, and Albuquerque. The heaviest rainfall occurred over northeast New Mexico during the evening and overnight hours.  Raton Crews Airport picked up a record 3.99 inches of rainfall with an impressive 4.91 inches at Mosquero.  Incredibly, no reports of damage or flash flooding were received across northeastern New Mexico. However, rises were noted on several area creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall dumped 3.99 inches at the Raton Crews Airport.  This was the 2nd highest 24-hour precipitation total reported at the Raton Crews Airport dating back to 1941.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and attendant surface frontal boundary sagged southward from Colorado and across portions of eastern New Mexico while broad high pressure began to build in from the west. This boundary allowed for an increase in moisture across the eastern plains as well as a focus for shower and thunderstorm development. Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed during the mid afternoon hours across the higher terrain areas and moved eastward. By early evening time some lines of strong storms had moved across the far eastern plains. One of these strong thunderstorm clusters created a wind gust that damaged the roof of a residence in Melrose.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SKYWARN Spotter observed partial roof damage to a structure from strong storms that struck the area. However, preexisting structural issues may have contributed to the damage. Radar estimated wind gusts to around 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm that developed over the Albuquerque Foothills around 3pm on the 13th produced very heavy rainfall within the Embudo Arroyo drainage. In two separate events, a teenage girl and a homeless man were swept away by the flood waters within Embudo Arroyo. Several people witnessed said individuals in the urban arroyo channel and notified authorities. The National Weather Service had notified the Fire Department about the heavy rainfall and rescue teams were already positioned to  assist the individuals to safety. This event resulted in a significant amount of local media coverage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 16-year-old girl and a homeless man were swept into the Embudo Arroyo channel in separate incidents. They were both rescued safely after a harrowing experience.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed along the high terrain of central New Mexico by mid to late afternoon on the 10th.  Weak west to southwest steering flows forced this activity into the Rio Grande Valley between 4 and 5pm.  Activity became clustered around the area from Algodones south to Bernalillo and Corrales.  A particularly heavy rainfall-producing thunderstorm slowly moved through Corrales, producing significant flooding and widespread impacts to the village.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports from local volunteer observers in the area reported rainfall amounts as high as 0.94 inches in less than one-half hour, with additional rainfall after the main storm. The Corrales Fire Department responded to numerous reports of flooding. Several homes and businesses were inundated with mud and water. A couple of cars were nearly half submerged by water and debris. Trees were also downed from flooding. A preliminary storm survey by local emergency management officials indicated between 15 and 20 roads were damaged and/or completely destroyed, many of which were paved. A couple residents were isolated from the village and could not travel from the area for several hours due to the flooding. Mail delivery services were temporarily suspended in some areas of Corrales because of road damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper trough resulted in a cold frontal passage, and high winds in the  Guadalupe Mountains.

Most areas received near to above average precipitation during the month of August. The month started out with a large area in Extreme(D3) drought, but by the end of the month, just a small area in Hidalgo and Grant Counties had some areas of Extreme(D3) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month began with Extreme(D3) drought over all but the far north where Severe(D2) drought was noted. on August 5th, the entire zone was in Severe(D2) drought. The Jornada Range Cooperative Observer reported 2.24 inches of precipitation which was 0.28 inches above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most areas received near to above average precipitation during the month of August. The month started out with a large area in Extreme(D3) drought, but by the end of the month, just a small area in Hidalgo and Grant Counties had some areas of Extreme(D3) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme(D3) drought was observed for all areas except the far southeast where Severe(D2) drought was noted. On August 5th, the entire zone was put into Severe(D2) drought. The New Mexico State University Cooperative Observer reported 2.11 inches of precipitation which was 0.24 inches above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful back door cold front surged southwest across the eastern plains during the afternoon of the 14th then plowed west through the Rio Grande Valley.  The combination of deep moisture and strong lift associated with the front along with weak steering flows beneath the upper level high produced widespread heavy rainfall. A couple severe thunderstorms erupted over the area given the presence of strong vertical wind shear.  The most severe flooding occurred well after sunset when training thunderstorms around the Espanola area produced prolific rainfall amounts.  A CoCoRaHS observer to the northwest of Espanola reported an impressive 4.88 inches of rainfall from this storm.  Major flash flooding was observed around Espanola.  A total of 8 homes were damaged by flood waters and numerous vehicles were abandoned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level high centered over the Four Corners gradually shifted east during the morning hours. This allowed monsoonal moisture to come up from the south and southwest. Showers and thunderstorms initially developed over the higher terrain of northern and western New Mexico during the early afternoon. A cluster of showers with embedded moderate to heavy rain then expanded during the evening hours.  A water-conveying channel was over-topped causing water to flow over service roads in the area, just west of the Pueblo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported  dangerous flows overtopped the intersection of Indian Service Route 601 and 602 on Santa Clara Pueblo. Access to the creek was blocked by authorities.

Quarter size hail was reported around Raton.  Rainfall amounts between 1 and 2 inches were reported over much of Union County.  No damage or widespread flooding was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lincoln.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the second day in a mutli-day significant severe weather outbreak across eastern New Mexico. A back door cold front moved into the northeast and east central portions of the state throughout the morning then nudged up against the central mountain chain by the afternoon hours. Deep moisture and instability aided in the development of supercell thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours along the east slopes of the central mountain chain that moved into the eastern plains. Large, damaging hail, strong winds, heavy downpours, and tornadoes were reported. Hail ranging from 2 to 4 inches in diameter caused significant damage to windows and vehicles as well as livestock and homes around Corona and Clovis.  A tornado was also reported in Corona with only damage to open rangeland.  Another thunderstorm that moved through Ranchvale and Clovis produced widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chaser reported tornado on the ground for 10 minutes.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief break from heavy rainfall across the state, yet another strong and very moist back door frontal boundary sagged southwest into New Mexico.  The upper level high pressure center was generally over far southeast New Mexico allowing for moist southerly flow in the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere to impact the area as well.  Slow-moving thunderstorms produced torrential rainfall across many areas during the early to mid afternoon hours, including Santa Fe, Rio Rancho, and Albuquerque. The heaviest rainfall occurred over northeast New Mexico during the evening and overnight hours.  Raton Crews Airport picked up a record 3.99 inches of rainfall with an impressive 4.91 inches at Mosquero.  Incredibly, no reports of damage or flash flooding were received across northeastern New Mexico. However, rises were noted on several area creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash Flooding on State Highway 56 and 412 at mile markers 10 and 12.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported that heavy rainfall in the area from Cochiti south to Kewa Pueblo, San Felipe Pueblo, Algodones, and Rio Rancho resulted in numerous reports of flooding.  All arroyos around San Felipe Pueblo were reported to be running fast and full.  Residents of Sile were cut off due to flooding across a low water crossing that formed due to repeated flooding from the Las Conchas burn scar.  A low water crossing also formed by the Las Conchas flooding was reported fast and full along Tribal Road 84 near Cochiti. No extensive damage was reported but EM felt this event was flash flood worthy.  Heavy rainfall also created minor flooding around Rio Rancho. The area of Algodones the received flooding on the 23rd was also likely impacted by more heavy rainfall.  Damage is estimated for road surfaces near low water crossings and clean up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon hours across much of the high terrain of central New Mexico.  This activity moved slowly west and congealed into a large complex of heavy rainfall within the Rio Grande Valley between Kewa Pueblo, Algodones, and Placitas.  Torrential rainfall from storms that moved slowly through Algodones produced flash flooding along a conservancy ditch to the east of town.  The levee was breached and significant amounts of water and mud spread into nearby areas.  Damage was reported to the irrigation structure, several homes, and the Algodones fire station.  Another area of heavy thunderstorms that developed south of Taos impacted the community of Talpa.  A clogged culvert caused water to burst into a nearby community, displacing several residents from their homes.  No significant damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid runoff from an estimated 2 to 4 inch downpour resulted in a breached levee that flooded several homes across from the Algodones fire station.  The fire department was pumping water away from Highway 313.  Water was still being pumped out of 2 homes the following morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms with heavy rainfall developed along the Continental Divide early on the 29th and pushed slowly east toward the Rio Grande Valley through the evening hours.  Several strong cells produced hail over portions of the area. Nickel size hail and heavy rain impacted Arroyo Seco.  Hail was also reported near Las Vegas and Moriarty.  A powerful storm near Abiquiu produced torrential rainfall, flooding, and damaging hail.

Strong thunderstorms with heavy rainfall developed along the Continental Divide early on the 29th and pushed slowly east toward the Rio Grande Valley through the evening hours.  Several strong cells produced hail over portions of the area. Nickel size hail and heavy rain impacted Arroyo Seco.  Hail was also reported near Las Vegas and Moriarty.  A powerful storm near Abiquiu produced torrential rainfall, flooding, and damaging hail.

Isolated slow-moving thunderstorms over the Ruidoso area produced heavy rainfall and penny sized hail.  An outflow boundary that surged west into the Rio Grande Valley produced strong winds on the east side of Albuquerque.  A few pine trees were toppled by strong winds near the small community of San Antonio and Interstate 25.

Vigorous thunderstorms developed across the southeast plains and the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains during the late afternoon to early evening hours on the 21st.  Two large scale outflow boundaries that surged out of these areas of convection collided over Quay County by late evening.  Training thunderstorms developed in the area between Tucumcari, Porter, and San Jon between about 8pm and 9pm, then again from about 1030pm to 130am on the 22nd.  Rainfall estimates from Cannon AFB and Amarillo radars indicated a large portion of this area received 5 to 7 inches. A report from the periphery of this heavy rainfall near San Jon indicated 3.05 inches. No reports of flash flooding were received from the area of heavy rainfall around Porter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State police dispatcher reported that she received 3.05 inches of rain at her residence during the overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated slow-moving thunderstorms over the Ruidoso area produced heavy rainfall and penny sized hail.  An outflow boundary that surged west into the Rio Grande Valley produced strong winds on the east side of Albuquerque. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three pine trees were toppled by gusty canyon winds near San Antonio and Interstate 25.  No damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread slow-moving heavy thunderstorms that moved eastward across the eastern plains very early on the morning of the 2nd produced torrential rainfall from De Baca County eastward into Curry and Roosevelt counties.  Two to 4 inches of rain were reported across much of this area, including as much as 4.5 inches in Clovis.  Many roads around Fort Sumner became impassable due to high water.  Clovis experienced dangerous flash flooding with significant street flooding.  A car along a section of 14th street was submerged in 8 feet of water.  Standing water and impacts to roadways across the region continued throughout the day, well after storms had exited the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Fort Sumner reported 4.50 inches of rainfall overnight. The Fort Sumner COOP observer reported 3.17 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A human-caused wildfire that started near the Asaayi Lake and Bowl Canyon recreation area east of Crystal, New Mexico burned for nearly 2 weeks. The communities of Naschitti and Sheep Springs were partially evacuated. The preliminary number of displaced residents was approximately 500. A total of 24 residences and 4 outbuildings were destroyed. The monetary value of damages were estimated for this event.  There were three established shelters. The fire consumed around 14712 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Incident management reported 14,712 acres consumed in the Chuska Mountains to the east of Crystal, New Mexico. At least 24 structures and 4 outbuildings were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just after midnight on the morning of the 16th, a line of strong storms crossed south eastward across Interstate 40 near Santa Rosa. This storm produced two lightning strikes that hit a rest area and gas station causing power failures. ||The greatest amount of atmospheric moisture available to date for the summer of 2014 was in place across much of central New Mexico.  Light northwest steering flow aloft beneath an upper level high pressure center building over southwestern New Mexico allowed for locally heavy rainfall.  An area of thunderstorms developed over the southern slopes of the Jemez Mountains forced development farther south into the Albuquerque Metro Area.  These storms developed from northwest to southeast and continually trained over the center of Albuquerque for nearly an hour.  The heaviest rainfall impacted the area from near Montano and Coors southeast across the UNM area, the Albuquerque Sunport, the area around Juan Tabo and Copper, and Mesa del Sol. Significant standing water was reported along Coors Boulevard. Strong flows created impassible conditions along Washington Avenue between Indian School and Constitution.  A frightening YouTube video was posted of the Embudo Canyon flood channel with water battering water beneath of overpass along Interstate 40.  The water was only about 5 feet away from pouring out onto Interstate 40. The most significant flooding occurred around Mesa del Sol where several homes took in water several inches deep.  Many yards in the area reported around 6 inches of standing water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several new homes in Mesa del Sol with several inches of water inside.  Many yards with up to 6 inches of standing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft was centered over southwestern New Mexico while a cold front backed in from the northeast. This front ushered in additional moisture to this area as well as acted as a focus for thunderstorm development. Isolated strong to severe storms developed into the late afternoon and early morning hours and moved to the south. A severe storm developed just north of the town of Anton Chico producing heavy rains, large hail and destructive wind. A mobile home was flipped over and destroyed by this severe storm.  A couple hours later a cluster of cells developed over the Sandia Mountains also creating heavy downpours and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mobile home was flipped and subsequently destroyed due to strong winds from a passing severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While the overall coverage of showers and thunderstorms began to diminish on the 17th, the storms that did manage to develop were strong to severe.  A storm that impacted the area around Black Lake produced penny sized hail that covered the ground.  Another persistent area of heavy rainfall from strong storms produced significant flooding around La Madera.  A foot bridge was washed out near a local farm and washed several miles downstream on the Rio Tusas.  Another strong storm blew up in the Santa Fe foothills and produced penny sized hail.  The strongest storm of the day developed around Las Vegas during the very late afternoon hours and moved south for several hours through the high plains over eastern New Mexico before dissipating along the Capitan Mountains.  Residents are Encino reported strong rotation in this thunderstorm with many suspected reports of funnel clouds.  Radar data suggested hail as large as baseballs possible with this storm.  A rural mesonet site recorded a peak wind gust to near 80 mph as the storm blasted through.  One final round of funnel clouds and quarter size hail was reported east of Encinoso as the storm was dissipating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging from nickel to quarter size was reported on Highway 246 by a storm chaser.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active and persistent pattern maintained a heightened threat for flash flooding across much of the state. An upper level high was centered over portions of northern New Mexico and southern Colorado. A disturbance rotating around the upper high slowly shifted from west central to north central areas throughout the overnight hours. Weak steering flow aloft combined with a very moist airmass in place fueled slow-moving thunderstorms that produced very heavy rainfall and flash flooding. Showers and thunderstorms began developing during the late morning hours on the 28th over the Southwest Mountains, the Lower Rio Grande Valley, the West Central Plateau and the Northern Mountains. Coverage expanded and intensified into the afternoon hours with small, heavy rain-producing cells. Activity slowly began to decrease into the evening hours. However, by 3am, a large swath of light to moderate rain with embedded heavy precipitation had developed across the central highlands. Thunderstorms began training over the area from Santa Rosa eastward to near Newkirk. This swath of precipitation slowly moved eastward throughout the early morning hours finally subsiding by 11am the 29th. Due to ground already saturated from the previous days rainfall, significant flooding occurred across portions of Guadalupe and De Baca counties.  In particular, major flash flooding was reported within the Alamogordo Creek drainage.  River flooding was also reported along the Pecos River near Puerto de Luna.  Radar estimated storm total accumulations ranged from 7 to 13 inches in this area. These torrential rains washed out the supports underneath train tracks near Santa Rosa leaving the track suspended in air. Numerous other reports of flooding including asphalt up-ended and roads closed due to flowing water occurred in east central areas. ||Converging outflow boundaries from storms in Western New Mexico created enough rotation to produce small rope like funnel clouds as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Owners of a large ranch south southeast of Newkirk reported an incredible 7.05 inches of rainfall from torrential rains overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms slowly shifted east across eastern New Mexico very early in the morning on the 25th.  Most areas picked up heavy rainfall and small hail. However,  Cannon AFB reported penny size hail for a few minutes.

Dry showers and thunderstorms that developed along the Continental Divide during the late afternoon hours pushed slowly east toward the Albuquerque metro area.  One of these showers produced a dry microburst that blew a semi-trailer truck over on Interstate 40 near Atrisco Vista.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Semi-trailer truck blown over on Interstate 40 near Atrisco Vista from microburst wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist backdoor cold front pushed southwest across the eastern plains and through the central mountains. Strong instability and sufficient low level shear set the stage for a significant round of severe weather across eastern New Mexico. Storms began to form during the early afternoon hours with many cells becoming severe by midday. Several storms produced large hail, strong winds, and a few tornadoes.  A powerful storm near Maxwell produced hen egg size hail and significant damage to cars and windows. A supercell thunderstorm that developed near Ranchvale and progressed across the north side of Clovis produced a tornado in Ranchvale and widespread wind and hail damage in Clovis. An impressive 79mph wind gust was reported at Cannon Air Force Base. The NMSU Agricultural Science Center suffered major damage to crops, buildings, and vehicles from hail. Thunderstorms continued in the northeast portion of the state into the following morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter observed funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving upper level low pressure system over the Great Basin steered rich monsoonal moisture into New Mexico. This system generated widespread showers and thunderstorms along and west of the central mountain chain. Since the system was slow -moving and moisture rich, some storms produced locally heavy rainfall. One such thunderstorm in the Santa Fe area produced enough rain to strand a vehicle at an intersection. Rainfall amounts around the Santa Fe area were near 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local Broadcast media shared a photo of a car that was stranded due to flooding at 5th Street and St. Michaels Dr. in Santa Fe. The driver drove into deep water that had accumulated on roadways from short duration, very heavy rainfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to 6 feet of water from the Alamogordo Creek drainage over Highway 156. Pavement was stripped. Half a mile of fence line was tore out due to the flooding. A bunkhouse located near the water crossing took on water damage and water filled up the basement. Two water retention ponds were over-topped. Part of the Pacific Railroad tracks were left dangling because the base was washed out from the heavy rains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level high pressure system was centered over southwest New Mexico. A strong and moist low level easterly push increased moisture in central areas. Afternoon showers and thunderstorms developed across the higher terrain of northern and south central New Mexico. Storms slowly moved to the southeast. A few strong storms developed across the east as favorable vertical wind shear profiles helped to organize thunderstorms.  Flash flooding was reported within the Bandelier National Monument. Other strong storms produced wind damage around Dexter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper level pattern continued to be dominated by broad high pressure over the state. An upper level disturbance tracked eastward across central and northern New Mexico triggering showers and thunderstorms over much of the area. Moisture remained abundant with precipitable water vales nearly 150% of normal. Showers and thunderstorms developed during the late morning hours across central portions of the state. These storms slowly moved eastward throughout the afternoon. During the late afternoon hours, a thunderstorm developed in far northwest Bernalillo County and intensified as it moved east southeast into the Albuquerque South Valley.  This storm dropped over one inch of rainfall in less than 45 minutes causing significant flooding in Albuquerque's South Valley. Another isolated strong thunderstorm near Bernal in San Miguel county produced a well-defined funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported water up to the body frames of vehicles with multiple feet of water at 98th Street and Dennis Chavez.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent back door cold front that shifted west across eastern New Mexico early on the 8th plowed through the Rio Grande Valley and became stationary along the Continental Divide.  The upper level high was centered over central New Mexico and resulted in very weak steering winds over the region.  A thunderstorm that developed over the Las Conchas burn scar produced flash flooding within Frijoles Canyon.  Another storm that developed farther southwest toward Jemez Springs also produced flash flooding along Highway 4. Other very heavy rain-producing storms were noted to the north of Cuba and around Mount Taylor however no flash flooding was reported.  A brief severe storm near Santa Fe produced quarter sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One foot of water with mud and debris over Highway 4.

Other very heavy rain-producing storms were noted to the north of Cuba and around Mount Taylor however no flash flooding was reported.  A brief severe storm near Santa Fe produced quarter sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wall of water 4.5 feet surged down Frijoles Canyon within Bandelier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent back door cold front that shifted west across eastern New Mexico early on the 8th plowed through the Rio Grande Valley and became stationary along the Continental Divide.  The upper level high was centered over central New Mexico and resulted in very weak steering winds over the region.  A thunderstorm that developed over the Las Conchas burn scar produced flash flooding within Frijoles Canyon.  Another storm that developed farther southwest toward Jemez Springs also produced flash flooding along Highway 4. Other very heavy rain-producing storms were noted to the north of Cuba and around Mount Taylor however no flash flooding was reported.  A brief severe storm near Santa Fe produced quarter sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 550 and Highway 537 were impacted and the Fire Chief reported 1.5 to 2 of rain to the local Emergency Manager. There was some damage to county roads with strong flows in the arroyos. Two feet of standing water was observed on the asphalt of roads in some locations. However, no roads were closed and all debris was cleared by the following day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft centered over and just east of the state allowed deep monsoonal moisture to work north to south fueling shower and thunderstorm activity to develop across the higher terrain of New Mexico.  The clockwise steering flow moved thunderstorms slowly toward the south-southeast and onto the adjacent northeast highlands and far northeast plains. A brief funnel cloud was reported from a strong storm near Las Vegas.

Another round of monsoonal showers and thunderstorms developed across the higher terrain throughout the afternoon hours. By mid to late afternoon a few isolated cells formed near the Upper Tularosa Valley and produced thunderstorm wind gusts ranging from 58 to 67 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WSMR Mesonet site located at Oscura Range Camp recorded a gust to 58 mph during the afternoon as strong thunderstorms moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the second day in a mutli-day significant severe weather outbreak across eastern New Mexico. A back door cold front moved into the northeast and east central portions of the state throughout the morning then nudged up against the central mountain chain by the afternoon hours. Deep moisture and instability aided in the development of supercell thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours along the east slopes of the central mountain chain that moved into the eastern plains. Large, damaging hail, strong winds, heavy downpours, and tornadoes were reported. Hail ranging from 2 to 4 inches in diameter caused significant damage to windows and vehicles as well as livestock and homes around Corona and Clovis.  A tornado was also reported in Corona with only damage to open rangeland.  Another thunderstorm that moved through Ranchvale and Clovis produced widespread wind damage.

A storm survey for this storm proved difficult due to the inaccessibility of private land.  Any damage would have most likely been sustained to scrub brush and juniper trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving upper level low pressure system over the Great Basin pulled abundant moisture north and west into New Mexico.  The combination of deep moisture and instability over eastern New Mexico set the stage for severe thunderstorms beginning by late morning through the late afternoon hours.  Several reports of large hail, strong winds, and heavy rainfall were received.  A large tree in Roswell was blown over onto nearby power lines and a car due to the strong winds.  Pea to quarter size hail accumulated 6 to 12 inches along Interstate 25 between Watrous and Wagon Mound, creating numerous traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State police reported that a man was struck by lightning and killed while riding his motorcycle along U.S. Highway 84 just west of Cimarron at mile post 308 Friday afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist backdoor cold front pushed southwest across the eastern plains and through the central mountains. Strong instability and sufficient low level shear set the stage for a significant round of severe weather across eastern New Mexico. Storms began to form during the early afternoon hours with many cells becoming severe by midday. Several storms produced large hail, strong winds, and a few tornadoes.  A powerful storm near Maxwell produced hen egg size hail and significant damage to cars and windows. A supercell thunderstorm that developed near Ranchvale and progressed across the north side of Clovis produced a tornado in Ranchvale and widespread wind and hail damage in Clovis. An impressive 79mph wind gust was reported at Cannon Air Force Base. The NMSU Agricultural Science Center suffered major damage to crops, buildings, and vehicles from hail. Thunderstorms continued in the northeast portion of the state into the following morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported downed telephone pole from thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60mph or greater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While the overall coverage of showers and thunderstorms began to diminish on the 17th, the storms that did manage to develop were strong to severe.  A storm that impacted the area around Black Lake produced penny sized hail that covered the ground.  Another persistent area of heavy rainfall from strong storms produced significant flooding around La Madera.  A foot bridge was washed out near a local farm and washed several miles downstream on the Rio Tusas.  Another strong storm blew up in the Santa Fe foothills and produced penny sized hail.  The strongest storm of the day developed around Las Vegas during the very late afternoon hours and moved south for several hours through the high plains over eastern New Mexico before dissipating along the Capitan Mountains.  Residents are Encino reported strong rotation in this thunderstorm with many suspected reports of funnel clouds.  Radar data suggested hail as large as baseballs possible with this storm.  A rural mesonet site recorded a peak wind gust to near 80 mph as the storm blasted through.  One final round of funnel clouds and quarter size hail was reported east of Encinoso as the storm was dissipating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major footbridge was reported to have washed away on the Owl Peak Farm in La Madera. Both the Rio Ojo Caliente and the Tusas River rose to flood levels. The Rio Ojo Caliente showed a river rise from 6 cfs to 1120 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active and persistent pattern maintained a heightened threat for flash flooding across much of the state. An upper level high was centered over portions of northern New Mexico and southern Colorado. A disturbance rotating around the upper high slowly shifted from west central to north central areas throughout the overnight hours. Weak steering flow aloft combined with a very moist airmass in place fueled slow-moving thunderstorms that produced very heavy rainfall and flash flooding. Showers and thunderstorms began developing during the late morning hours on the 28th over the Southwest Mountains, the Lower Rio Grande Valley, the West Central Plateau and the Northern Mountains. Coverage expanded and intensified into the afternoon hours with small, heavy rain-producing cells. Activity slowly began to decrease into the evening hours. However, by 3am, a large swath of light to moderate rain with embedded heavy precipitation had developed across the central highlands. Thunderstorms began training over the area from Santa Rosa eastward to near Newkirk. This swath of precipitation slowly moved eastward throughout the early morning hours finally subsiding by 11am the 29th. Due to ground already saturated from the previous days rainfall, significant flooding occurred across portions of Guadalupe and De Baca counties.  In particular, major flash flooding was reported within the Alamogordo Creek drainage.  River flooding was also reported along the Pecos River near Puerto de Luna.  Radar estimated storm total accumulations ranged from 7 to 13 inches in this area. These torrential rains washed out the supports underneath train tracks near Santa Rosa leaving the track suspended in air. Numerous other reports of flooding including asphalt up-ended and roads closed due to flowing water occurred in east central areas. ||Converging outflow boundaries from storms in Western New Mexico created enough rotation to produce small rope like funnel clouds as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water from the Alamogordo Creeek drainage between 3 and 4 feet deep over Highway 84. Around 300 yards wide. Highway shut down by State Police.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of monsoonal showers and thunderstorms developed across the higher terrain throughout the afternoon hours. By mid to late afternoon a few isolated cells formed near the Upper Tularosa Valley and produced thunderstorm wind gusts ranging from 58 to 67 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WSMR Mesonet site located at Phillips Hill recorded a peak gust to 67 mph during the late afternoon as strong thunderstorms moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of monsoonal showers and thunderstorms developed across the higher terrain throughout the afternoon hours. By mid to late afternoon a few isolated cells formed near the Upper Tularosa Valley and produced thunderstorm wind gusts ranging from 58 to 67 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway within Peralta Canyon was washed out and recently built culverts were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level high pressure system was centered over southwest New Mexico. A strong and moist low level easterly push increased moisture in central areas. Afternoon showers and thunderstorms developed across the higher terrain of northern and south central New Mexico. Storms slowly moved to the southeast. A few strong storms developed across the east as favorable vertical wind shear profiles helped to organize thunderstorms.  Flash flooding was reported within the Bandelier National Monument. Other strong storms produced wind damage around Dexter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just after midnight on the morning of the 16th, a line of strong storms crossed south eastward across Interstate 40 near Santa Rosa. This storm produced two lightning strikes that hit a rest area and gas station causing power failures. ||The greatest amount of atmospheric moisture available to date for the summer of 2014 was in place across much of central New Mexico.  Light northwest steering flow aloft beneath an upper level high pressure center building over southwestern New Mexico allowed for locally heavy rainfall.  An area of thunderstorms developed over the southern slopes of the Jemez Mountains forced development farther south into the Albuquerque Metro Area.  These storms developed from northwest to southeast and continually trained over the center of Albuquerque for nearly an hour.  The heaviest rainfall impacted the area from near Montano and Coors southeast across the UNM area, the Albuquerque Sunport, the area around Juan Tabo and Copper, and Mesa del Sol. Significant standing water was reported along Coors Boulevard. Strong flows created impassible conditions along Washington Avenue between Indian School and Constitution.  A frightening YouTube video was posted of the Embudo Canyon flood channel with water battering water beneath of overpass along Interstate 40.  The water was only about 5 feet away from pouring out onto Interstate 40. The most significant flooding occurred around Mesa del Sol where several homes took in water several inches deep.  Many yards in the area reported around 6 inches of standing water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two lightning bolts just a few seconds apart made direct hits  to the TravelCenters of America just after midnight on July 16th. The strikes caused a power surge which fried circuitry and temporarily knocked 17 fuel pumps out of service. The pumps were not restored to service until the late afternoon during that day as electricians worked to repair the damage caused by the strikes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft was positioned over western areas of the state, elongated from Nevada to Mexico. Remnant monsoonal moisture as well as warming daytime temperatures allowed for the development of showers and thunderstorms throughout the late afternoon into the evening. A line of storms that developed in northern New Mexico, nearly parallel to Interstate 25, extending from Raton to south of Las Vegas, created heavy rainfall. This line of showers with embedded thunderstorms created heavy rainfall in a short amount of time which caused flash flooding at the Capulin Volcano National Monument as a mudslide ran over a road.  The storm affected the area for just over one hour with storm total estimates of one inch in 30 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense rainfall caused a mudslide over Volcano Rd. at the Capulin Volcano National Monument. The road was closed the following day for debris removal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most areas received near to above average precipitation during the month of August. The month started out with a large area in Extreme(D3) drought, but by the end of the month, just a small area in Hidalgo and Grant Counties had some areas of Extreme(D3) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone remained in Severe(D2) drought through the entire month. The Cooperative Observer at Gila Hot Springs received 3.27 inches of precipitation which was 0.25 inches above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance rapidly moved east over central New Mexico and interacted with mid level moisture over the area to produce numerous dry showers and thunderstorms.  Activity developed early in the morning over western areas then pushed quickly east into the Rio Grande Valley from Albuquerque south to Socorro.  Wind gusts at the Albuquerque Sunport reached 52 mph.  The strongest winds with this activity impacted the Los Lunas area where a semi-trailer truck was blown over on Interstate 25.  A small hay shed collapsed and the sheet metal roofing blew nearly 200 yards away.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outflow winds from a virga shower blew over a semi-trailer truck along Interstate 25 in Los Lunas.  A small hay shed also collapsed and the sheet metal roofing blew up to 200 yards away.

A back door cold front moved into the northeast and east central portions of the state throughout the morning then nudged up against the central mountain chain by the afternoon hours. Deep moisture and instability aided in the development of supercell thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours along the east slopes of the central mountain chain that moved into the eastern plains. Large, damaging hail, strong winds, heavy downpours, and tornadoes were reported. Hail ranging from 2 to 4 inches in diameter caused significant damage to windows and vehicles as well as livestock and homes around Corona and Clovis.  A tornado was also reported in Corona with only damage to open rangeland.  Another thunderstorm that moved through Ranchvale and Clovis produced widespread wind damage.

A surface low pressure system was positioned across portions of eastern New Mexico. In the upper levels of the atmosphere,  high pressure was sprawled across southern and portions of eastern New Mexico with an elongated trough of lower pressure stretched from southwest Canada to northern California. Southwest flow dominated the weather pattern with some afternoon gusty winds and showers across the high terrain. During the late afternoon and early evening hours,  dry, high-based showers tracked northeast across the central highlands near McIntosh that resulted in a dry microburst that overturned a semi-trailer truck as well as damaged other structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CQC ASOS at Clines Corners recorded a peak wind gust to 63 MPH.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Raton Crews Airport recorded a non-thunderstorm wind gust to 58 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple local news outlets reported that a wedding tent collapsed due to high winds injuring guests. The winds were outflow winds from a storm to the north of the location. Gusts estimated to be 60 + mph. The tent was a 40x80 ft tent which collapsed onto a wedding reception party during dinner service. Two women were taken to the hospital for non-life threatening injuries while 6 others sustained minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough approached New Mexico and spun off a weak disturbance that moved northward up the Rio Grande Valley. This disturbance brought ample moisture which fueled showers over much of western New Mexico. As the day progressed, the shower activity shifted and expanded eastward. Daytime heating and increased instability triggered some embedded thunderstorms in this activity. One of these storms across eastern New Mexico produced a 58 mph wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust to 58mph was recorded at the Clovis AWOS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Cannon Air Force Base recorded a thunderstorm wind gust to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure continued to dominate New Mexico.  Monsoonal moisture being drawn up from the south fueled numerous afternoon showers and thunderstorms. A thunderstorm near Los Lunas produced an outflow boundary that raced to the north and west.  As the boundary reached the Albuquerque Sunport,  a gust to 58 mph was recorded by the ASOS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ABQ ASOS recorded a wind gust to 58 mph as outflow from thunderstorms to the south reached the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful back door cold front surged southwest across the eastern plains during the afternoon of the 14th then plowed west through the Rio Grande Valley.  The combination of deep moisture and strong lift associated with the front along with weak steering flows beneath the upper level high produced widespread heavy rainfall. A couple severe thunderstorms erupted over the area given the presence of strong vertical wind shear.  The most severe flooding occurred well after sunset when training thunderstorms around the Espanola area produced prolific rainfall amounts.  A CoCoRaHS observer to the northwest of Espanola reported an impressive 4.88 inches of rainfall from this storm.  Major flash flooding was observed around Espanola.  A total of 8 homes were damaged by flood waters and numerous vehicles were abandoned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptionally heavy rainfall report of 4.88 inches northwest of Espanola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm that developed along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains produced abundant hail along the Interstate 25 corridor between Wagon Mound and Springer.  Up to six inches of pea size hail impacted Interstate 25 around mile marker 400, which prompted DOT officials to plow the interstate.  One semi-trailer truck jack-knifed on the ice-covered road.  The storm strengthened further and produced quarter size hail just to the east of Wagon Mound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front, or a cold front that moves from northeast to southwest, moved into the northeast corner of the state providing an increase in moisture as well as  directional wind shear thanks to surface winds from the east and southeast topped by westerly winds aloft. Storms began to develop across the higher terrain locations of northern and central New Mexico during the early afternoon hours and drifted east into adjacent plains. The mix of increased moisture, wind shear, and instability, led to the strongest storms in the northeast corner of the state. At least one of these storms became severe with quarter size hail reported near the Colorado state line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to penny size hail reported by trained spotter.

A moist backdoor cold front pushed southwest across the eastern plains and through the central mountains. Strong instability and sufficient low level shear set the stage for a significant round of severe weather across eastern New Mexico. Storms began to form during the early afternoon hours with many cells becoming severe by midday. Several storms produced large hail, strong winds, and a few tornadoes.  A powerful storm near Maxwell produced hen egg size hail and significant damage to cars and windows. A supercell thunderstorm that developed near Ranchvale and progressed across the north side of Clovis produced a tornado in Ranchvale and widespread wind and hail damage in Clovis. An impressive 79mph wind gust was reported at Cannon Air Force Base. The NMSU Agricultural Science Center suffered major damage to crops, buildings, and vehicles from hail. Thunderstorms continued in the northeast portion of the state into the following morning.

Numerous thunderstorms with strong winds, tornadoes, large hail, and heavy rainfall impacted New Mexico on this third in a series of significant severe weather days.  Thunderstorms developed along the central mountain chain early in the afternoon then moved slowly east southeast across the plains.  Several small short-lived tornadoes developed along the high plains, in particular around Maxwell, Wagon Mound, Valmora, Springer, and Encinoso.  The Encinoso tornado destroyed 2 homes and severely damaged 2 others.  Many reports of quarter to baseball size hail were also reported with these storms.  The largest hail reported was around 3 inches in diameter east of Interstate 25 near Valmora.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail with lots of quarter size hail as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported a vehicle that was washed off the road due to heavy rains in Rio Rancho on Northern Blvd. near 10th street. The young driver attempted to pass through fast-moving water during the early morning hours of the 15th and the vehicle was swept off the road and buried up to its wheels in mud. The road also sustained damage due to the undercutting of the water. Large chunks of asphalt were chipped off. Rain began in the area just before 10pm and did not stop until close to 7 am the following morning. Mostly light stratiform rain continued over the area with embedded heavy cells. KABX storm total precipitation estimated ranged from 2.5 to 3.2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms with heavy rainfall developed along the Continental Divide early on the 29th and pushed slowly east toward the Rio Grande Valley through the evening hours.  Several strong cells produced hail over portions of the area. Nickel size hail and heavy rain impacted Arroyo Seco.  Hail was also reported near Las Vegas and Moriarty.  A powerful storm near Abiquiu produced torrential rainfall, flooding, and damaging hail.

Several strong cells produced hail over portions of the area. Nickel size hail and heavy rain impacted Arroyo Seco.  Hail was also reported near Las Vegas and Moriarty. EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Mexico DOT reported that Highway 283 was closed near San Geronimo due to flash flooding.  A CoCoRaHS observer in the area reported 2.81 inches of rainfall from this storm with strong runoff.  2.30 inches of that fell in just 30 minutes with hail.  Highway 283 was reopened the following morning around 11am.
