EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some improvement in drought conditions was noted across much of Sierra County in July, but conditions worsened over Hidalgo, Grant and Dona Ana Counties. Precipitation varied greatly across the area with some locations receiving over 200% of normal while others areas were slightly below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme(D3) drought conditions remained in place across the entire zone during the month of July. The Redrock Cooperative Observer reported 2.22 inches of precipitation which was 0.25 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms that developed as a result of outflow boundary collisions over the west side of Albuquerque produced locally heavy rainfall and hail.  A particularly strong cell on Albuquerque's northwest mesa produced brief pea to nickel sized hail and minor street flooding. Elsewhere, heavy rainfall over the Little Bear burn scar created a small rock slide that blocked one lane of Ski Run Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The storm system that impacted portions of New Mexico with snow and strong winds on the 22nd exited into the Great Plains on the 23rd. A potent jet stream on the back side of this storm system combined with a strong surface pressure gradient along the Texas state line to produce areas of high wind. The strongest winds developed from Clayton to Clovis where gusts between 55 and 59 mph were reported. Numerous roads across far northeastern New Mexico remained closed as blowing snow created treacherous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clayton airport reported sustained winds of 44 mph and gusts to 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The storm system that impacted portions of New Mexico with snow and strong winds on the 22nd exited into the Great Plains on the 23rd. A potent jet stream on the back side of this storm system combined with a strong surface pressure gradient along the Texas state line to produce areas of high wind. The strongest winds developed from Clayton to Clovis where gusts between 55 and 59 mph were reported. EVENT_NARRATIVE: CWOP site briefly reported gusts of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pockets of near to above normal rainfall were observed over southern, western and northeast New Mexico, but a majority of the state experienced below normal precipitation.  Widespread wetting rain fell over central and especially western New Mexico from the 8th through 10th associated with the remnants of a tropical cyclone with another precipitation-producing system the 12th to 13th, including a dusting of snow on the high peaks of the northern mountains.  Another active and moderately wet period occurred from the 18th through 22nd followed by an overall dry period the remainder of the month.   As a result,  there was very little change in drought status during the month though extreme drought across the northwest corner of the state decreased slightly to less than 3 percent.  Approximately 30 percent of the state was classified in severe drought or worse,  primarily the northern and western halves.  The southeast quarter of New Mexico to include much of Lincoln, Chaves, Eddy and Lea counties were classified as abnormally dry if not better.

A cluster of thunderstorms that developed as a result of outflow boundary collisions over the west side of Albuquerque produced locally heavy rainfall and hail.  A particularly strong cell on Albuquerque's northwest mesa produced brief pea to nickel sized hail and minor street flooding. Elsewhere, heavy rainfall over the Little Bear burn scar created a small rock slide that blocked one lane of Ski Run Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall over the Little Bear burn scar produced a small rock slide that closed 1 lane of Ski Run Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure that had dominated the weather pattern several days prior shifted to the west. The monsoonal moisture plume shifted slightly to the northeast with general storm motions of 10 to 15 mph. Lingering overnight showers and thunderstorms from the previous night left abundant cloud cover over the area leading to a later start to shower and thunderstorm activity. Showers began to develop into the mid afternoon hours with the threat for heavy rain continuing due to the moisture and slow moving storms. Cells developed in western areas into the mid afternoon moving to the north east. As the storms continued to develop into the evening hours multiple cells trained over the Church Rock area leading to flash flooding. Though storm total and one hour rates were not extremely high the continuous rain over the already saturated ground and the flood prone location caused flooding. There were reports of water in houses as well as the water main breaking due to maximum capacity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local news reported that the city of Church Rock was flooded after heavy rainfall. Multiple roads were washed out with access to homes cut off. A few homes were flooded in the area and that the main water line broke under the pressure of all the rushing water. The community was still cleaning up three days after the event.

The southeast quarter of New Mexico to include much of Lincoln, Chaves, Eddy and Lea counties were classified as abnormally dry if not better.

Pockets of near to above normal rainfall were observed over southern, western and northeast New Mexico, but a majority of the state experienced below normal precipitation.  Widespread wetting rain fell over central and especially western New Mexico from the 8th through 10th associated with the remnants of a tropical cyclone with another precipitation-producing system the 12th to 13th, including a dusting of snow on the high peaks of the northern mountains.  Another active and moderately wet period occurred from the 18th through 22nd followed by an overall dry period the remainder of the month. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first significant storm system of the fall season raced southeast out of the Pacific Northwest and into the central Rockies on the 12th. The combination of powerful upper level winds and a potent surface cold front produced widespread strong winds over New Mexico. Enough mid-level moisture was present to fuel scattered showers and thunderstorms over central and eastern New Mexico. The convective activity enhanced the transport of stronger winds aloft to the surface and resulted in several reports of wind gusts near 70 mph. Some very light high elevation snowfall was also reported around the Sangre de Cristo Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A passing virga shower produced a severe wind gust to 66 mph at an APRS site near Placitas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnant circulation of Hurricane Simon slowly moved north along Baja California and into southwestern Arizona through the first week of October. This tropical system induced a regional-scale fetch of moist southerly flow over the southwestern United States for several days through the 10th. Overall this system produced beneficial rainfall over several days and did not result in any widespread heavy rainfall and flash flooding. However, one particularly persistent area of rain and thunderstorms over northwestern New Mexico on the 9th did result in flash flooding around La Plata. Minor flooding was reported along Highway 4 from rainfall in the Jemez Mountains. A strong thunderstorm over eastern New Mexico produced nickel sized hail around Tucumcari.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused a culvert to fail and travel was impacted along Highway 170 north of La Plata. Water flooded at least one home near the culvert.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture in place over the area on deep southeasterly flow interacted with a strong upper level low pressure system that dropped south out of Colorado. The richest atmospheric moisture available for the entire summer period was present over the area. Storms developed by mid to late afternoon on the 1st over northern New Mexico then pushed very slowly south through the evening and predawn hours on the 2nd.  The first area of torrential rainfall impacted the area around Embudo and Pilar.  Storms developed over the Albuquerque Metro Area during the late evening and produced flash flood emergency-level flooding within the area from UNM campus into downtown on a busy Friday night.  Numerous water rescues were reported. Several businesses reported water inside with just about the entire area of Central Avenue closed. Mud and rock slides created numerous lane closures around area roadways.  An overnight deluge washed out ballasts along a portion of the Rail Runner tracks within Waldo Canyon, resulting in suspended service for the entire day Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Mexico DOT reported an overnight deluge caused ballasts under portions of the Rail Runner line within Waldo Canyon to wash out.  Service was suspended for the entire day on Saturday for repairs.

A powerful upper level storm system and attendant Pacific cold frontal boundary raced across New Mexico during the afternoon of the 26th.  This front delivered a potent blast of strong southwest to west winds, colder temperatures, and blowing dust to just about the entire state.  Snow even impacted the northern high terrain where generally 3 to 6 inches was reported.  The strongest winds occurred along and just behind the cold frontal boundary where numerous locations reported peak speeds around 60 to 65 mph.  Blowing dust reduced visibilities in many areas of eastern New Mexico below 3 miles for several hours. Fortunately no significant damage was reported with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based off the graph produced by the Magdalena Ridge Observatory a peak gust to 110 mph was recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was moisture coming into the region from Tropical Storm Odile over northern Mexico/southern New Mexico.  This made the air mass very moist.  Upper level disturbances were moving over the area from Odile which increased atmospheric lift.  The air was also unstable, especially for areas that received sunshine during the day.  These conditions allowed for rain and thunderstorms to develop across areas that had already received good rainfall allowing for flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Lea County and produced flash flooding in Hobbs.  Around six inches of water was reported running over several roads in Hobbs.  At the time of the report, no roads were closed.  No damage was reported so the cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper low continued to affect the area by bringing a chance|for showers and thunderstorms.  The area near Red Bluff Reservoir received 3 to 4 inches of rain overnight.  Areas of Southern Eddy County had already seen significant rainfall over the last several days.  The additional rainfall only aggravated ongoing flooding concerns across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains that occurred overnight as well as saturated soils lead to continued flooding of roads across Southern Eddy County.  A couple of those road closures included Whites City Road, Dog Town Road, and McDonald Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was moisture coming into the region from Tropical Storm Odile over northern Mexico/southern New Mexico.  This made the air mass very moist.  Upper level disturbances were moving over the area from Odile which increased atmospheric lift.  The air was also unstable, especially for areas that received sunshine during the day.  These conditions allowed for rain and thunderstorms to develop across areas that had already received good rainfall allowing for flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Eddy County and produced flash flooding near Whites City.  Two vehicles were washed off of Whites City Road about 0.9 miles east northeast of Carlsbad Caverns Visitor Center.  The two vehicles were trying to cross a low lying section of the road when the flood waters caused the vehicles to lose control.  The driver of the first vehicle was able to get out of the vehicle.  The driver of the second vehicle swam to safety.  The passenger of the second vehicle was reported missing and the body was later found.  This flooding resulted in one fatality.  The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation was below normal in the west, mostly near normal in central areas and generally above normal in the east especially the far southeast.  Approximately 28 percent of western and northern New Mexico was classified in severe drought or worse during the month, with little significant change from late October.  The northwest plateau and highlands continued to experience the most significant and persistent drought conditions while south-central and southeast areas of the state were classified as abnormally dry if not better.

Abundant moisture in place over the area on deep southeasterly flow interacted with a strong upper level low pressure system that dropped south out of Colorado. The richest atmospheric moisture available for the entire summer period was present over the area. Storms developed by mid to late afternoon on the 1st over northern New Mexico then pushed very slowly south through the evening and predawn hours on the 2nd.  The first area of torrential rainfall impacted the area around Embudo and Pilar.  Storms developed over the Albuquerque Metro Area during the late evening and produced flash flood emergency-level flooding within the area from UNM campus into downtown on a busy Friday night.  Numerous water rescues were reported. Several businesses reported water inside with just about the entire area of Central Avenue closed. Mud and rock slides created numerous lane closures around area roadways.  An overnight deluge washed out ballasts along a portion of the Rail Runner tracks within Waldo Canyon, resulting in suspended service for the entire day Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mudslide along the access road to Interstate 25 near Mountain Rd.  Damage estimated for landscaping property loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remains of Tropical Storm Odile were moving across the region as they rotated around the circulation of an upper ridge.  A circulation was also located over the central Permian Basin which was allowing rain to move across the same areas.  There was plentiful moisture across the area from Odile as well as from tropical storm Polo.  Good upper level lift was also present from Odile.  Heating from breaks in the cloud cover allowed for instability to increase.  This moisture, lift and instability allowed for rain and thunderstorms to continue moving over the same areas leading to more flash flooding for West Texas and southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Eddy County and produced flash flooding near Whites City.  Highway 62/180 was closed due to high water about 7 miles southwest of Whites City.  The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Odile and an associated mid-level circulation were still over the region.  The airmass was very moist, and lift was enhanced by upslope low-level winds across southeast New Mexico and the higher terrain of West Texas.  These conditions resulted in rain and thunderstorms across the area, and flash flooding continued as moderate to heavy rain moved over areas with previously saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Eddy County and produced flash flooding near Artesia.  Two feet of running water was at the junction of U.S. Highway 285 and State Road 2 north of Artesia making the road impassable.  The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the second day in a mutli-day significant severe weather outbreak across eastern New Mexico. A back door cold front moved into the northeast and east central portions of the state throughout the morning then nudged up against the central mountain chain by the afternoon hours. Deep moisture and instability aided in the development of supercell thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours along the east slopes of the central mountain chain that moved into the eastern plains. Large, damaging hail, strong winds, heavy downpours, and tornadoes were reported. Hail ranging from 2 to 4 inches in diameter caused significant damage to windows and vehicles as well as livestock and homes around Corona and Clovis.  A tornado was also reported in Corona with only damage to open rangeland.  Another thunderstorm that moved through Ranchvale and Clovis produced widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of hail damage just north of Pecos from significant hailstones up to hen egg size in diameter. Several homes sustained window, skylight, and roof damage, including dents to a metal roof, as well as substantial damage to at least three vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first significant storm system of the fall season raced southeast out of the Pacific Northwest and into the central Rockies on the 12th. The combination of powerful upper level winds and a potent surface cold front produced widespread strong winds over New Mexico. Enough mid-level moisture was present to fuel scattered showers and thunderstorms over central and eastern New Mexico. The convective activity enhanced the transport of stronger winds aloft to the surface and resulted in several reports of wind gusts near 70 mph. Some very light high elevation snowfall was also reported around the Sangre de Cristo Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnant circulation of Hurricane Simon slowly moved north along Baja California and into southwestern Arizona through the first week of October. This tropical system induced a regional-scale fetch of moist southerly flow over the southwestern United States for several days through the 10th. Overall this system produced beneficial rainfall over several days and did not result in any widespread heavy rainfall and flash flooding. However, one particularly persistent area of rain and thunderstorms over northwestern New Mexico on the 9th did result in flash flooding around La Plata. Minor flooding was reported along Highway 4 from rainfall in the Jemez Mountains. A strong thunderstorm over eastern New Mexico produced nickel sized hail around Tucumcari.

Pockets of near to above normal rainfall were observed over southern, western and northeast New Mexico, but a majority of the state experienced below normal precipitation.  Widespread wetting rain fell over central and especially western New Mexico from the 8th through 10th associated with the remnants of a tropical cyclone with another precipitation-producing system the 12th to 13th, including a dusting of snow on the high peaks of the northern mountains.  Another active and moderately wet period occurred from the 18th through 22nd followed by an overall dry period the remainder of the month.   As a result,  there was very little change in drought status during the month though extreme drought across the northwest corner of the state decreased slightly to less than 3 percent.  Approximately 30 percent of the state was classified in severe drought or worse,  primarily the northern and western halves.  The southeast quarter of New Mexico to include much of Lincoln, Chaves, Eddy and Lea counties were classified as abnormally dry if not better.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low passing to the north of New Mexico steered a polar jet overhead. The right entrance region of the jet crossed south central to east central areas increasing dynamic forcing and the threat for severe weather as abundant moisture as well as instability were also present. By early afternoon a long line of showers and thunderstorms had developed across the northern mountains with another line extending from Raton down the eastern plains to Carrizozo. Many of these embedded storms became severe with large hail, high wind, and even a tornado reported.  The activity continued into the late night hours across Quay and Roosevelt counties before exiting east into Texas right around midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado traveled slightly north of due east through complex terrain of the Lazy Triangle Anchor Ranch, passing less than one quarter mile north of the ranch headquarters located near the intersection of U.S. Highway 64/84. The tornado traveled through terrain ranging from 7500 feet to 9800 feet in elevation. Numerous if not hundreds of large diameter pine trees and cedars were snapped and/or twisted and subsequently uprooted along the first one to one and a half miles. An old long-standing chapel dating back to the 1940s was destroyed. A 6-ft long 4x6 beam was tossed 150 yards. The main ranch residence suffered considerable damage including complete removal of it's pitched roof that was carried 50 yards to the east northeast. A bunkhouse and other storage buildings sustained significant damage. Winds were estimtated at 110 mph, the upper end of EF-1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In stark contrast to northern New Mexico,  where precipitation was below normal during September 2014,  southern areas of the state experienced well above if not record-breaking precipitation owing to the remnants of former Hurricane Odile that impacted the region for nearly a week from the 15th through 22nd.  South-central and southeast New Mexico were especially hard hit with major flash floods, mainstem flooding along the Pecos River and significant damage to homes and roads.  It was officially the 5th wettest September on record at Roswell.  Following Odile's remnants, a round of strong to severe thunderstorms struck the eastern plains on the 25th with large hail and damaging winds followed by the final severe weather event of the monsoon season on the 29th with large hail and damaging winds for parts of northern and western New Mexico, and even a tornado west of Chama!  Reservoirs along the Pecos River in southeast New Mexico were replenished by the Odile rainfall, including Brantley and Red Bluff. Significant improvement in drought conditions were noted in southern New Mexico, and much of southeast New Mexico were drought-free by month's end. However, the Odile rainfall, while beneficial, was not enough to eliminate 3+ years of drought in other parts of New Mexico. A band of moderate drought (D1) remained from southwest to south central New Mexico, with an oval of severe drought (D2) in the southwest corner of the state.  Areas to the north received very little to no rain from Odile and severe to extreme drought (D2-D3) was expanded in northwest New Mexico. USDA reported that, on a statewide basis, soil moisture and pastures and rangeland improved in New Mexico, with the values decreasing to 52% of topsoil and 56% of subsoil short or very short of moisture and 32% of pasture and rangeland rated in poor to very poor condition.

Above normal rainfall across all of southern New Mexico lead to improvements in drought conditions for many areas. At the end of the month, no location in the region was still in Extreme(D3) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month started with Extreme(D3) drought for the southeastern portion of the zone and Severe(D2) drought for the remainder of the zone. At the end of the month, the northern and eastern portions of the zone were in Severe(D2) drought and the remainder was down to Moderate(D1) drought. The Redrock Cooperative Observer reported 2.62 inches of precipitation which was 1.05 inches above normal.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month started out with Severe(D2) drought for the southeast and northwest portions of the zone, while the remainder of the area was in Extreme(D3) drought. At the end of the month, the southeast and northwest sections were in Moderate(D1) drought and the rest of the zone was in Severe(D2) drought. The Antelope Wells Cooperative Observer reported 4.55 inches of precipitation which was 2.89 inches above normal.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe(D2) drought was reported over the far southern areas of the zone to start the month with Moderate(D1) drought or better for the remainder of the area. On September 23rd, all areas were put into Moderate(D1) drought or Abnormally Dry(D0) conditions. The Truth or Consequences Airport reported 3.98 inches of precipitation which was 2.41 inches above normal.

Widespread wetting rain fell over central and especially western New Mexico from the 8th through 10th associated with the remnants of a tropical cyclone with another precipitation-producing system the 12th to 13th, including a dusting of snow on the high peaks of the northern mountains.  Another active and moderately wet period occurred from the 18th through 22nd followed by an overall dry period the remainder of the month.   As a result,  there was very little change in drought status during the month though extreme drought across the northwest corner of the state decreased slightly to less than 3 percent.  Approximately 30 percent of the state was classified in severe drought or worse,  primarily the northern and western halves.  The southeast quarter of New Mexico to include much of Lincoln, Chaves, Eddy and Lea counties were classified as abnormally dry if not better.

The southeast quarter of New Mexico to include much of Lincoln, Chaves, Eddy and Lea counties were classified as abnormally dry if not better.

In stark contrast to northern New Mexico,  where precipitation was below normal during September 2014,  southern areas of the state experienced well above if not record-breaking precipitation owing to the remnants of former Hurricane Odile that impacted the region for nearly a week from the 15th through 22nd.  South-central and southeast New Mexico were especially hard hit with major flash floods, mainstem flooding along the Pecos River and significant damage to homes and roads.  It was officially the 5th wettest September on record at Roswell.  Following Odile's remnants, a round of strong to severe thunderstorms struck the eastern plains on the 25th with large hail and damaging winds followed by the final severe weather event of the monsoon season on the 29th with large hail and damaging winds for parts of northern and western New Mexico, and even a tornado west of Chama!  Reservoirs along the Pecos River in southeast New Mexico were replenished by the Odile rainfall, including Brantley and Red Bluff. Significant improvement in drought conditions were noted in southern New Mexico, and much of southeast New Mexico were drought-free by month's end. However, the Odile rainfall, while beneficial, was not enough to eliminate 3+ years of drought in other parts of New Mexico. A band of moderate drought (D1) remained from southwest to south central New Mexico, with an oval of severe drought (D2) in the southwest corner of the state.  Areas to the north received very little to no rain from Odile and severe to extreme drought (D2-D3) was expanded in northwest New Mexico. USDA reported that, on a statewide basis, soil moisture and pastures and rangeland improved in New Mexico, with the values decreasing to 52% of topsoil and 56% of subsoil short or very short of moisture and 32% of pasture and rangeland rated in poor to very poor condition.

A monsoon moisture surge containing the remnants of two tropical systems moved into the state from the south and southwest. An elongated ridge of high pressure across the southern U.S. aided in the flow of the monsoonal moisture northward into New Mexico. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the higher terrain across the west and central areas. By late afternoon into the early evening hours scattered to numerous slow moving showers and thunderstorms had developed. Some cells became severe across the north with burn scar flooding occurring across the south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the hail was nickel size or smaller but for a time there were some very near quarter size to slightly larger. Some standing water also and moderate winds.

High pressure building over the western plains sent the remnant moisture of Hurricane Odile, which had passed over the region a few days earlier, back into far west Texas and southwestern New Mexico. A weak upper trough was moving into the southwestern U.S. which aided in providing lift to the region. Heavy rain caused several road closures and a vehicle to be washed away near Cliff and flooding in downtown Truth or Consequences.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads in Truth or Consequences were flooded as well as the Animas Creek. No water made it into the stores around the downtown area however.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dryline developed along and east of the central mountain chain after a weak backdoor cold front pushed through the plains during the morning hours. Monsoonal moisture continued to move northward into portions of southern and eastern New Mexico. The increased moisture along with upslope flow and daytime surface heating resulted in a large area of showers and thunderstorms across the central and south central highlands during the afternoon hours.  Favorable vertical wind profiles and more than sufficient instability allowed for the development of severe storms that produced large hail. The slow-moving nature of the storms produced torrential rainfall and flash flooding across the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm that developed over the Albuquerque International Sunport shifted slowly west into Albuquerque's South Valley late on the 15th.  This storm produced very heavy rainfall, strong winds, and a large dust devil or gustnado within the south valley corridor. Local broadcast media reported downed tree limbs and power lines with power outages for several hours. Residents indicated minor damage to landscaping with grills and other outdoor items tossed around.  However, no significant widespread flooding or major damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large dust devil reported in south valley area.

A prolonged period of moderate to locally heavy rainfall associated with the remnant moisture from Hurricane Odile impacted much of southern and eastern New Mexico between the 17th and 20th. Many areas from the upper Gila region of Catron County eastward across Socorro and Lincoln counties into the eastern plains reported 4 day rainfall amounts of 2 to nearly 5 inches. This prolonged heavy rainfall event and abundant remnant moisture set the stage for flash flooding as a second upper disturbance approached from northwestern Mexico on the 21st. A large area of heavy rainfall shifted very slowly north across Lincoln and Chaves counties resulting in flash flooding. A persistent area of torrential rainfall later in the day around Cimarron also produced significant flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reporting that people were displaced from homes in Cimarron as heavy rains led creeks to overflow. Water was also reported in some homes. The village sustained extensive damage to roads from over 5 inches of rain and creek overflowing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough was positioned over the Great Basin, with strong southwesterly winds aloft. One disturbance rounded the base of the trough and clipped northern New Mexico Sunday resulting in strong winds. Widespread shower and thunderstorm then moved over the area and aided stronger winds. The strongest gusts were convectively-aided and topped out at 72 mph at Clayton.

An upper level impulse moving very slowly northeast over New Mexico after multiple days of heavy rainfall produced areas of flash flooding yet again. A wave of heavy rainfall early in the day over Chaves County produced flash flooding along U.S. 285 north of Roswell. The most serious flash flooding occurred later in the day around Santa Fe as a slow-moving thunderstorm dropped more than 2 inches of rainfall. A 50-year-old man in the Santa Fe River was washed away and drowned by flood waters late in the evening of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Writer from the Santa Fe newspaper reported that there was damage to E.J. Martinez Elementary School from flash flooding. It was reported that there was damage to 3 classrooms, the gym, and an office.

The southeast quarter of New Mexico to include much of Lincoln, Chaves, Eddy and Lea counties were classified as abnormally dry if not better.

An upper level impulse moving very slowly northeast over New Mexico after multiple days of heavy rainfall produced areas of flash flooding yet again. A wave of heavy rainfall early in the day over Chaves County produced flash flooding along U.S. 285 north of Roswell. The most serious flash flooding occurred later in the day around Santa Fe as a slow-moving thunderstorm dropped more than 2 inches of rainfall. A 50-year-old man in the Santa Fe River was washed away and drowned by flood waters late in the evening of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported running over Highway 285 at mile marker 137 north of Roswell. The road was closed due to high water and cleared for travel 5 hours later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In stark contrast to northern New Mexico,  where precipitation was below normal during September 2014,  southern areas of the state experienced well above if not record-breaking precipitation owing to the remnants of former Hurricane Odile that impacted the region for nearly a week from the 15th through 22nd.  South-central and southeast New Mexico were especially hard hit with major flash floods, mainstem flooding along the Pecos River and significant damage to homes and roads.  It was officially the 5th wettest September on record at Roswell.  Following Odile's remnants, a round of strong to severe thunderstorms struck the eastern plains on the 25th with large hail and damaging winds followed by the final severe weather event of the monsoon season on the 29th with large hail and damaging winds for parts of northern and western New Mexico, and even a tornado west of Chama!  Reservoirs along the Pecos River in southeast New Mexico were replenished by the Odile rainfall, including Brantley and Red Bluff. Significant improvement in drought conditions were noted in southern New Mexico, and much of southeast New Mexico were drought-free by month's end. However, the Odile rainfall, while beneficial, was not enough to eliminate 3+ years of drought in other parts of New Mexico. A band of moderate drought (D1) remained from southwest to south central New Mexico, with an oval of severe drought (D2) in the southwest corner of the state.  Areas to the north received very little to no rain from Odile and severe to extreme drought (D2-D3) was expanded in northwest New Mexico. USDA reported that, on a statewide basis, soil moisture and pastures and rangeland improved in New Mexico, with the values decreasing to 52% of topsoil and 56% of subsoil short or very short of moisture and 32% of pasture and rangeland rated in poor to very poor condition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist west-northwest flow over the region behind a prolonged stretch of impressively heavy rainfall set the stage for more thunderstorms with locally heavy rainfall. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of NM 24 were washed out between mile markers 46 and 48 north of Dunken. The road had to be closed.

Localized flooding was also reported with heavy rainfall on top of saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gust of 55 to 60 mph with outflow winds and blowing dust. This was reported from the Interstate 40 welcome center just inside the NM border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level low pressure system was positioned over portions of southeast New Mexico. This disturbance combined with cooling temperatures and adequate low level moisture destabilized the area enough for the development of isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms. One of these afternoon thunderstorms across the east produced large hail within Chaves County.

An upper level low passing to the north of New Mexico steered a polar jet overhead. The right entrance region of the jet crossed south central to east central areas increasing dynamic forcing and the threat for severe weather as abundant moisture as well as instability were also present. By early afternoon a long line of showers and thunderstorms had developed across the northern mountains with another line extending from Raton down the eastern plains to Carrizozo. Many of these embedded storms became severe with large hail, high wind, and even a tornado reported.  The activity continued into the late night hours across Quay and Roosevelt counties before exiting east into Texas right around midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Albuquerque ASOS recorded a thunderstorm wind gust from the south at 62 mph during the afternoon hours of the 29th.

Significant improvement in drought conditions were noted in southern New Mexico, and much of southeast New Mexico were drought-free by month's end. However, the Odile rainfall, while beneficial, was not enough to eliminate 3+ years of drought in other parts of New Mexico. A band of moderate drought (D1) remained from southwest to south central New Mexico, with an oval of severe drought (D2) in the southwest corner of the state.  Areas to the north received very little to no rain from Odile and severe to extreme drought (D2-D3) was expanded in northwest New Mexico. USDA reported that, on a statewide basis, soil moisture and pastures and rangeland improved in New Mexico, with the values decreasing to 52% of topsoil and 56% of subsoil short or very short of moisture and 32% of pasture and rangeland rated in poor to very poor condition.

An upper level low passing to the north of New Mexico steered a polar jet overhead. The right entrance region of the jet crossed south central to east central areas increasing dynamic forcing and the threat for severe weather as abundant moisture as well as instability were also present. By early afternoon a long line of showers and thunderstorms had developed across the northern mountains with another line extending from Raton down the eastern plains to Carrizozo. Many of these embedded storms became severe with large hail, high wind, and even a tornado reported.  The activity continued into the late night hours across Quay and Roosevelt counties before exiting east into Texas right around midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was mostly pea size with a few quarter size stones.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In stark contrast to northern New Mexico,  where precipitation was below normal during September 2014,  southern areas of the state experienced well above if not record-breaking precipitation owing to the remnants of former Hurricane Odile that impacted the region for nearly a week from the 15th through 22nd.  South-central and southeast New Mexico were especially hard hit with major flash floods, mainstem flooding along the Pecos River and significant damage to homes and roads.  It was officially the 5th wettest September on record at Roswell.  Following Odile's remnants, a round of strong to severe thunderstorms struck the eastern plains on the 25th with large hail and damaging winds followed by the final severe weather event of the monsoon season on the 29th with large hail and damaging winds for parts of northern and western New Mexico, and even a tornado west of Chama!  Reservoirs along the Pecos River in southeast New Mexico were replenished by the Odile rainfall, including Brantley and Red Bluff. Significant improvement in drought conditions were noted in southern New Mexico, and much of southeast New Mexico were drought-free by month's end. However, the Odile rainfall, while beneficial, was not enough to eliminate 3+ years of drought in other parts of New Mexico. A band of moderate drought (D1) remained from southwest to south central New Mexico, with an oval of severe drought (D2) in the southwest corner of the state.  Areas to the north received very little to no rain from Odile and severe to extreme drought (D2-D3) was expanded in northwest New Mexico. USDA reported that, on a statewide basis, soil moisture and pastures and rangeland improved in New Mexico, with the values decreasing to 52% of topsoil and 56% of subsoil short or very short of moisture and 32% of pasture and rangeland rated in poor to very poor condition.
Above normal rainfall across all of southern New Mexico lead to improvements in drought conditions for many areas. At the end of the month, no location in the region was still in Extreme(D3) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe(D2) drought conditions continued for the entire month. The Gila Hot Springs Cooperative Observer reported 2.55 inches of precipitation which was 0.56 inches above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A monsoon moisture surge containing the remnants of two tropical systems moved into the state from the south and southwest. An elongated ridge of high pressure across the southern U.S. aided in the flow of the monsoonal moisture northward into New Mexico. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the higher terrain across the west and central areas. By late afternoon into the early evening hours scattered to numerous slow moving showers and thunderstorms had developed. Some cells became severe across the north with burn scar flooding occurring across the south.
Above normal rainfall across all of southern New Mexico lead to improvements in drought conditions for many areas. At the end of the month, no location in the region was still in Extreme(D3) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northern half of the zone remained in Severe(D2) drought during the month of September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level storm system and attendant Pacific cold frontal boundary raced across New Mexico during the afternoon of the 26th.  This front delivered a potent blast of strong southwest to west winds, colder temperatures, and blowing dust to just about the entire state.  Snow even impacted the northern high terrain where generally 3 to 6 inches was reported.  The strongest winds occurred along and just behind the cold frontal boundary where numerous locations reported peak speeds around 60 to 65 mph.  Blowing dust reduced visibilities in many areas of eastern New Mexico below 3 miles for several hours. Fortunately no significant damage was reported with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local news station reporting tree downed by high winds in the parking lot at Alameda and Coors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third day of strong winds impacted much of eastern New Mexico on the long-lived multi-day high wind event. Strong upper level flow continued to impact the region as the potent upper level storm system remained nearly stationary over the central plains states.  Northwest winds were the strongest along and east of the central mountain chain from Clines Corners to Tucumcari and Clovis where numerous locations reported peak wind gusts of 60 to 67 mph. No widespread significant damage was reported on this third day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melrose Range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet stream over New Mexico combined with a deepening surface trough over the plains to produce another round of strong gusty winds.  The strongest winds were observed along ridges of the central mountain chain and adjacent east slopes extending eastward across the high plains.  A few virga showers that developed over central and western New Mexico also resulted in localized strong gusts at Gallup and Albuquerque where gust up to 61 mph were reported.   Several other areas reported peak wind gusts to around 60 mph, including Clines Corners, Clovis, Sumner Lake and White Sands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sumner Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably potent upper level storm system moving southeast across the central Rockies pushed into the Four Corners region on the 11th.  A deep area of surface low pressure over the eastern plains tapped strong winds aloft with this storm system and produced areas of strong winds and some blowing dust over south central New Mexico. The strongest winds occurred within the area from Carrizozo to Ruidoso and Clines Corners where gusts between 58 and 67 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: White Sands observing sites at Stallion and Sulf reported peak winds between 61 and 62 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: White Sands observing sites at Phillips Hill reported 62 mph and Carrizozo weather net reported 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving back door cold front produced widespread windy conditions across the portions of eastern New Mexico late on the 16th.  As the front moved through Tucumcari a peak wind of 58 mph was reported.  Areas of blowing dust also occurred with visibilities as low as 3 miles.

As the unseasonably deep upper level storm system shifted east across northern New Mexico on the 27th, strong winds and areas of blowing dust redeveloped over much of the area.  West to northwest winds were the strongest along and east of the central mountain chain where numerous locations reported peak wind gusts of around 60 to 65 mph.  No significant damage was reported on this second day either. Areas of blowing dust occurred early in the day across far eastern New Mexico before pushing east into Texas.  Showers and colder air across northern and western New Mexico produced gusty winds in those areas again however speeds were lighter than on the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clovis Airport AWOS recorded sustained wind speeds ranging from 40 to 47 mph with a peak gust up to 58 mph.

This was the fourth and final day of long duration strong winds for the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gust to 59mph reported at Clayton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance moved across the southwest and south central mountains overnight on the 1st.  Rain and snow changed over to snow during the overnight hours as the precipitation expanded eastward into the east central plains. A localized area of heavier snow impacted the region around Corona where low level upslope flow interacted with the upper level wave.  Five to 7 inches of snow was reported along with tricky travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observers around Corona reported a localized maximum between 5 and 7 inches of new snow with slick travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a disturbance crossed to the north of the state, strong westerly winds aloft combined with above normal mixing heights and a deepening lee side trough resulted in strong and gusty winds across the Sangre de Cristo Mountains, northeast highlands and central highlands throughout the day. ||During the afternoon of the 16th a fire started south of Albuquerque in the Isleta Pueblo (Rec Fire). The cause for the fire is under investigation and was fully contained later that week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clines Corners ASOS recorded high winds during the morning of February 16, 2014. Sustained speeds ranging from 31-48 mph were recorded with a peak gust reaching 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The slow-moving upper level storm responsible for severe weather over New Mexico on the 22nd continued to impact the area on the 23rd.  Showers and thunderstorms developed over the high plains and moved east as a squall line through the early morning hours on the 24th.  Several reports of pea to quarter size hail, strong damaging winds, and flooding were received across eastern New Mexico. A motorcyclist was struck by lightning and killed near Cimarron.  The Roswell Airpark set an all-time record 24-hour rainfall amount of 4.44 inches dating back to 1949. Widespread flooding was reported particularly on the east side of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Albuquerque Sunport.  Numerous reports of pea to dime size hail were also reported with this storm across the metro area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably potent upper level storm system moving southeast across the central Rockies pushed into the Four Corners region on the 11th.  A deep area of surface low pressure over the eastern plains tapped strong winds aloft with this storm system and produced areas of strong winds and some blowing dust over south central New Mexico. The strongest winds occurred within the area from Carrizozo to Ruidoso and Clines Corners where gusts between 58 and 67 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both the Sierra Blanca airport and the Smokey Bear fire site reported peak winds up to 67 mph throughout the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving disturbance and associated jet streak aloft tracked southeastward toward New Mexico along the northern border. This resulted in strengthening westerly winds aloft as the day progressed. Winds aloft were 50-60 knots at 500mb with even stronger winds at mountain-top levels.  Wind speeds strengthened further overnight as the base of the trough passed to the north. In addition to the upper level wave, lee side troughing at the surface intensified over southeast Colorado during the evening extending into eastern New Mexico. Winds were initially focused across the northeast and higher terrain areas but shifted to areas along and immediately east of the central mountain chain into Tuesday morning. Many areas across the east experienced severely reduced visibilities in blowing dust. Winds gradually decreased into Tuesday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raton ASOS recorded high sustained wind speeds up to 45 mph with a peak gust to 62 mph during the late afternoon/early evening of March 17, 2014.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms that developed along the east slopes of the Sandia and Manzano Mountains early in the afternoon on the 21st pushed eastward and strengthened over the high plains.  One storm near Moriarty was able to produce a landspout tornado for about 10 minutes to the north of Interstate 40.  Another storm around Pastura produced quarter size hail. No damage was reported with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Landspout tornado just north of Interstate 40 near Moriarty on the ground for approximately 10 minutes.  No damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough was located over central New Mexico and moved east throughout the day.   Surface dew points were around 60 degrees Fahrenheit across portions of the Permian Basin.  An upper level disturbance and a weak dryline over the mountains of West Texas were the focus for thunderstorm development in Southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Lea County and produced an estimated 65 mph wind gust near Crossroads.  Some power poles were snapped as a result of this wind.  The cost of damage is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was over southern Arizona with upper level disturbances moving over New Mexico and the Big Bend region.  Residual outflow boundaries from thunderstorms the previous day were present across the area helping to provide additional lift.  There was a lot of moisture in the air so this aided in heavy rainfall.  The atmosphere became very unstable with an increase in heating, especially in the higher terrain.  This allowed for severe thunderstorms to develop across West Texas and southeast New Mexico with hail, flash flooding, and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Eddy County and produced hail north of Carlsbad.  There were some car dents as a result of this hail.  The cost of damage is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning-caused wildfire that started near the San Pedro Parks Wilderness area southeast of Coyote, New Mexico on the 25th burned for nearly 3 weeks. The fire smoldered for several days before blowing up on the 29th. Hikers and campers within the wilderness were forced to flee the fire otherwise no evacuations were noted. A total of 4 minor structures were destroyed and 1 damaged. The fire consumed around 3626 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Diego fire destroyed 4 minor structure and damaged one. Total acres burned was near 3626. No information was available on the structures that were burned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and precipitation moved in for the first weekend of March as a slow-moving moisture rich storm impacted northern and central New Mexico. A Pacific cold front moved into the state Saturday afternoon ushering in gusty southwest winds as well as a thick fetch of moisture ahead of a closed low off the coast of California. Strong winds aloft developed by the late morning and early afternoon as the strong winds began to mix down to the surface creating occasional gusts up to 60mph across the central mountain chain and eastward.  Moisture levels in the atmosphere were at or above 200% of normal. Precipitation began Saturday afternoon with snow accumulating across the Chuska and northern mountains.  Snow levels began above 7500 feet before dropping to 5500 feet across northwest areas Saturday evening.  The closed low weakened and crossed the state through the weekend with valley rain and high terrain snow reported across much of western, central and northern portions of the state.  A secondary peak in precipitation occurred Saturday night into early Sunday in association with a cold front across the northeast plains.  Precipitation tapered off from west to east through Sunday afternoon leaving behind dangerous driving conditions in eastern New Mexico due to the wintry mix of freezing rain, sleet and snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sierra Blanca AWOS recorded high sustained wind speeds ranging from 40 to 45 mph with a peak gust up to 59 mph during the afternoon of March 1, 2014.

In addition to the upper level wave, lee side troughing at the surface intensified over southeast Colorado during the evening extending into eastern New Mexico. Winds were initially focused across the northeast and higher terrain areas but shifted to areas along and immediately east of the central mountain chain into Tuesday morning. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weak and fast moving upper level disturbances crossed northern New Mexico Wednesday through Friday. Strong winds developed ahead of the first disturbance as lee side surface troughing developed in eastern Colorado Wednesday. Winds increased first across the western high terrain and areas along and east of the central mountain chain. Winds began to diminish after sunset across the western highlands and the east central and northeast plains. Winds persisted overnight along and east of the central mountain chain before strengthening further into Thursday. Winds tapered off after sunset Thursday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Las Vegas ASOS recorded peak gusts ranging from 60 to 62 mph during the afternoon and late evening of March 26, 2014 as well as the overnight hours into March 27, 2014.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Exceptional drought conditions (D4) eased somewhat across far eastern New Mexico during May 2014, thanks to a wet pattern that developed late in the month.  Elsewhere,  little change to the drought status was observed though most areas received near to above normal precipitation that provided some short-term relief. Specifically, a pair of weak disturbances managed to bring light precipitation to the north on the 6th and 7th. A stronger storm blasted through the state on the 11th with high winds and much cooler temperatures. A reinforcing shot of chilly air quickly followed on the 12th into the 13th. Accumulating snow fell in the northern mountains with late season freezing temperatures on the morning of the 13th and 14th.  An abrupt change then occurred as a storm to our west stalled and allowed Gulf of Mexico moisture to stream northwest into the state. Showers and thunderstorms erupted on the afternoon of the 21st across the far eastern plains, then expanded west on the 22nd and 23rd, and was widespread on the 24th and 25th.  Some severe weather and flooding across the eastern plains occurred on the 23rd and 24th. Roswell picked up 4.39 inches of rain during the early morning of the 23rd. The final week of the month was uneventful with a little hail on the 25th across the far eastern plains, and some showers and thunderstorms from the 28th through the 31st, none of which were severe.

A deep surge of low level moisture westward into central and eastern New Mexico interacted with an approaching upper level storm system from the Great Basin to produce another round of severe weather for the eastern plains.  Thunderstorms developed along a line over Socorro, Torrance, and Lincoln counties then advanced eastward into a bow echo over much of the plains.  The main impact from these storms was high winds.  Areas from Tucumcari south to Clovis reported peak winds between 60 and 70 mph.  No damage was reported.  Heavy rain and frequent lightning strikes accompanied these storms across the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: House Schoolnet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong mid and upper level winds and isolated virga showers across central New Mexico produced locally strong wind gusts.  The strongest winds occurred within southern Santa Fe and Torrance counties.  An observer in Stanley reported a peak wind gust to 61 mph, Sevilleta Wildlife Refuge 64 mph, and Clines Corners 59 mph.  No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stanley.

A slow-moving upper level low pressure system over the Great Basin pulled abundant moisture north and west into New Mexico.  The combination of deep moisture and instability over eastern New Mexico set the stage for severe thunderstorms beginning by late morning through the late afternoon hours.  Several reports of large hail, strong winds, and heavy rainfall were received.  A large tree in Roswell was blown over onto nearby power lines and a car due to the strong winds.  Pea to quarter size hail accumulated 6 to 12 inches along Interstate 25 between Watrous and Wagon Mound, creating numerous traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bottomless Lake State Park estimated wind gusts between 50 and 60mph as storm passed with pea size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong mid and upper level winds and isolated virga showers across central New Mexico produced locally strong wind gusts.  The strongest winds occurred within southern Santa Fe and Torrance counties.  An observer in Stanley reported a peak wind gust to 61 mph, Sevilleta Wildlife Refuge 64 mph, and Clines Corners 59 mph.  No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sevilleta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Exceptional drought conditions (D4) eased somewhat across far eastern New Mexico during May 2014, thanks to a wet pattern that developed late in the month.  Elsewhere,  little change to the drought status was observed though most areas received near to above normal precipitation that provided some short-term relief. Specifically, a pair of weak disturbances managed to bring light precipitation to the north on the 6th and 7th. A stronger storm blasted through the state on the 11th with high winds and much cooler temperatures. A reinforcing shot of chilly air quickly followed on the 12th into the 13th. Accumulating snow fell in the northern mountains with late season freezing temperatures on the morning of the 13th and 14th.  An abrupt change then occurred as a storm to our west stalled and allowed Gulf of Mexico moisture to stream northwest into the state. Showers and thunderstorms erupted on the afternoon of the 21st across the far eastern plains, then expanded west on the 22nd and 23rd, and was widespread on the 24th and 25th.  Some severe weather and flooding across the eastern plains occurred on the 23rd and 24th. Roswell picked up 4.39 inches of rain during the early morning of the 23rd. The final week of the month was uneventful with a little hail on the 25th across the far eastern plains, and some showers and thunderstorms from the 28th through the 31st, none of which were severe.

A slow-moving upper level low pressure system over the Great Basin pulled abundant moisture north and west into New Mexico.  The combination of deep moisture and instability over eastern New Mexico set the stage for severe thunderstorms beginning by late morning through the late afternoon hours.  Several reports of large hail, strong winds, and heavy rainfall were received.  A large tree in Roswell was blown over onto nearby power lines and a car due to the strong winds.  Pea to quarter size hail accumulated 6 to 12 inches along Interstate 25 between Watrous and Wagon Mound, creating numerous traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 3 foot diameter tree was felled and resulted in damagd to a nearby automobile and powerlines.  No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The slow-moving upper level storm responsible for severe weather over New Mexico on the 22nd continued to impact the area on the 23rd.  Showers and thunderstorms developed over the high plains and moved east as a squall line through the early morning hours on the 24th.  Several reports of pea to quarter size hail, strong damaging winds, and flooding were received across eastern New Mexico. A motorcyclist was struck by lightning and killed near Cimarron.  The Roswell Airpark set an all-time record 24-hour rainfall amount of 4.44 inches dating back to 1949. Widespread flooding was reported particularly on the east side of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage reported to cars and numerous windows shattered in area homes and businesses in Capitan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of showers and thunderstorms developed across New Mexico on the afternoon of the 24th.  Several areas of northern New Mexico reported heavy rainfall and small hail.  The strongest instability developed over the high plains where several reports of large hail were received.  The largest reported hailstone was up to golf ball sized around the Mesa area in northern Chaves County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One mile north of Mesa.

The last in a series of four days of thunderstorms impacted the eastern New Mexico as the core of a slow-moving upper level storm system shifted over the region.  The coverage of storms diminished on the fourth day. However,  penny to nickel sized hail was reported along the U.S. Highway 70 corridor around Kenna and Elkins.

An upper level low and trailing trough extending from Nevada into Arizona and western NM allowed for numerous storms to develop. Storms moved slowly from south to north and trained over the same areas. With many areas already seeing saturated ground from previous days of rainfall, the potential for flash flooding was exacerbated. Isolated cells began to develop during the late morning hours and increased in coverage and intensity by early afternoon. By 2pm strong cells had developed over the Tajique and Bandelier areas. Cells back-built over the Tajique area, with multiple hours of moderate to sometimes heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Santa Fe police reported that a lightning strike downed a power line and started a small brush fire at 1945 Cerrillos Road in Santa Fe. The fire is shown in a YouTube video burning for nearly 10 minutes before fire officials extinguished the blaze.

An upper level low and trailing trough extending from Nevada into Arizona and western NM allowed for numerous storms to develop. Storms moved slowly from south to north and trained over the same areas. With many areas already seeing saturated ground from previous days of rainfall, the potential for flash flooding was exacerbated. Isolated cells began to develop during the late morning hours and increased in coverage and intensity by early afternoon. By 2pm strong cells had developed over the Tajique and Bandelier areas. Cells back-built over the Tajique area, with multiple hours of moderate to sometimes heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water rose 5 to 6 feet in the Frijoles Creek at the Bandalier National Monument Visitors Center.

The combination of a very cold upper level storm system for mid May and a powerful cold front within central New Mexico produced widespread light snowfall in many areas along and north of Interstate 40.  Heavy snowfall occurred in the Sangre de Cristo Mountains where the strongest upslope flow and upper level forcing coincided.  Generally 8 to 11 inches of snow was reported in the higher terrain.  A peak wind gust of 63 mph was reported within the Upper Tularosa Valley as gap winds set up over the region.  Roadways become slick and icy across portions of northern New Mexico.  Highway 68 was closed south of Taos due to inclement weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer reported a snowfall amount up to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving upper level low pressure system over the Great Basin pulled abundant moisture north and west into New Mexico.  The combination of deep moisture and instability over eastern New Mexico set the stage for severe thunderstorms beginning by late morning through the late afternoon hours.  Several reports of large hail, strong winds, and heavy rainfall were received.  A large tree in Roswell was blown over onto nearby power lines and a car due to the strong winds.  Pea to quarter size hail accumulated 6 to 12 inches along Interstate 25 between Watrous and Wagon Mound, creating numerous traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Travel Center.

A dry month of June kept drought conditions generally in the Severe(D2) to Extreme(D3) categories for another month across Southwest New Mexico. All locations around the region reported below normal precipitation for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe(D2) drought continued through the month of June. The Cooperative Observer at Gila Hot Springs reported 0.26 inches of precipitation which was 0.37 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the Great Basin raced across New Mexico on the 13th.  Enough moisture and forcing along the frontal boundary combined to produce intense virga showers across many areas.  Powerful wind gusts were reported at numerous locations, including 67 mph at Gallup and Santa Fe.  A large tree was blown over near San Antonio as the cold front pushed through the area.  Meanwhile, gusty winds also impacted isolated areas of eastern New Mexico as the back door front plowed south into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry showers produced intense wind gusts up to 67 mph and blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture rich and very strong jet stream moved into the western United States, nosing into northern and central New Mexico Wednesday night January 29, into Thursday January 30, 2014. Upper level winds ranging from 140-160 knots helped to mix strong winds down to the surface along and east of the central mountain chain. The strongest winds initially impacted the higher ridges of the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains before spreading south and east. The focus for strong winds shifted to the south-central mountains and adjacent highlands into Friday, January 31.  Severe wind speeds were recorded across the state during this two day span with the ASOS at Raton airport recording a gust to 83 mph during the early morning hours of the 30th. ||Along with the severe wind the strong jet stream brought snow. As this deep fetch of Pacific moisture streamed across the state light rain and high terrain snow impacted northern border locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Magdalena Ridge Observatory was recording wind gust speeds above 58 mph for the majority of the evening of January 30, 2014.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough departed to the east of New Mexico as another wave followed closely on its heels into the weekend creating strong northwest winds. The second upper level wave shifted quickly across the northern and central mountains through Saturday afternoon. A surface low over eastern New Mexico deepened rapidly creating very windy conditions. Strong to very strong north to northwest winds developed by mid-day across western areas and expanded across the plains into the afternoon. Winds in the west began to weaken into the evening. The surface pressure gradient however remained strong throughout the overnight hours into the following day across the east. In addition to the strong winds were reduced visibilities in blowing dust across the east as well as snow in the north. Precipitation also impacted the state as the upper level trough interacted with a surface cold front. Snow amounts ranging from 2 to 6 inches were reported across locations in the northern mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both the Tucumcari ASOS as well as Mesonet site in Quay county recorded sustained wind speeds in the low forties during the early evening of March 15, 2014.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weak and fast moving upper level disturbances crossed northern New Mexico Wednesday through Friday. Strong winds developed ahead of the first disturbance as lee side surface troughing developed in eastern Colorado Wednesday. Winds increased first across the western high terrain and areas along and east of the central mountain chain. Winds began to diminish after sunset across the western highlands and the east central and northeast plains. Winds persisted overnight along and east of the central mountain chain before strengthening further into Thursday. Winds tapered off after sunset Thursday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter near Corona relayed a wind gust of 65 mph during the evening of March 26, 2014.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET/RED RIVER
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active spring weather scenario unfolded across northern and central New Mexico on the 5th and 6th as gusty winds, mountain snow, and valley showers and thunderstorms impacted the region. Unstable northwest flow and sufficient moisture over the region produced persistent snow showers over the northern high terrain. Seven to 9 inches of fresh snow was reported around Amalia, Red River and Taos Ski Valley.  A man photographing a dust devil near Moriarty was grazed by a lightning strike from a dry thunderstorm.  No reports of high winds were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous observations including ski sites and SNOTELs reported between 7 and 9 inches of snow, including Red River and Taos Ski Valley.  Difficult driving conditions were also reported across a large area of the northern higher terrain.

The cause for the fire is under investigation and was fully contained later that week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bartley RAWS recorded a high wind gust to 58 mph the afternoon of February 16, 2014.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front swept south across the east and a Pacific front entered the west. The base of an upper trough aloft tracked to the north of the state along the Colorado border. As the upper trough crossed eastward temperatures dropped and widespread rain changed over to snow across the northern half of the state. Gusty north to northwest winds also created reduced visibilities in snow and blowing snow across the northern highlands and plains. Accumulations occurred from the Sangre de Cristo's high county to the Raton Ridge and the northeast plains. Snow changed back over to rain as temperatures rose. Precipitation then ended over the north and began to taper off over the southern high country heading into Saturday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Santa Fe Ski site reporting 8 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough skirted the northern border with lee side troughing at the surface. Strong northwest winds developed during the morning across the central highlands as the wave moved east and daytime heating and mixing began. Winds had already begun to pick up during the overnight hours producing some mountain wave activity along the Sangre de Cristos and suspected at the Sandia and Manzanos as well. Wind speeds peaked in the afternoon with peak gusts nearing 60 mph. The northwesterly winds gave way to a strong back door cold front during the late afternoon. The front quickly plunged southward through the eastern plains continuing the high winds through the evening. Wind speeds gradually decreased into Wednesday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clines Corners ASOS recorded sustained winds to 40 mph during the early afternoon hours of the 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level storm system pushed across northern and central New Mexico on the 5th and 6th.  Cold air in place behind the persistent back cold frontal airmass provided lift for a band of mainly light to moderate snowfall over eastern New Mexico.  The heaviest snowfall actually occurred with strong upslope flow along the southwest facing areas of the Mogollon Rim where 2 to 8 inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various reports across the area indicated 2 to 8 inches of new snow impacted the area.  The heaviest snow fell along southwest facing slopes around Mogollon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a disturbance crossed to the north of the state, strong westerly winds aloft combined with above normal mixing heights and a deepening lee side trough resulted in strong and gusty winds across the Sangre de Cristo Mountains, northeast highlands and central highlands throughout the day. ||During the afternoon of the 16th a fire started south of Albuquerque in the Isleta Pueblo (Rec Fire). The cause for the fire is under investigation and was fully contained later that week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raton Airport ASOS recorded high winds during the afternoon of February 16, 2014. Sustained speeds in the upper thirties as well as a peak wind gust to 58mph were recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last in a series of upper level storm systems interacted with a pesky back door cold frontal boundary over central and eastern New Mexico to produce banded areas of heavy snowfall.  Many areas of northern and central New Mexico received measurable snowfall. However, the main impact areas occurred in a well defined snow band around the Albuquerque and Santa Fe metro areas as well as the south central mountains near Ruidoso. Difficult travel was reported across portions of the area however temperatures were warm enough in most of the Rio Grande Valley to mitigate severe impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports from various sources across the area indicated snowfall developed late on the 3rd then became heavy around sunrise on the 4th. Areas from Capitan to Ruidoso and Lincoln picked up 2 to 8 inches.  School closings and hazardous travel conditions were noted around the area.

Areas from Tesuque to Santa Fe and Lamy picked up 2 to 5 inches. Hazardous travel conditions were noted around the area with numerous accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level storm system and attendant Pacific cold frontal boundary raced across New Mexico during the afternoon of the 26th.  This front delivered a potent blast of strong southwest to west winds, colder temperatures, and blowing dust to just about the entire state.  Snow even impacted the northern high terrain where generally 3 to 6 inches was reported.  The strongest winds occurred along and just behind the cold frontal boundary where numerous locations reported peak speeds around 60 to 65 mph.  Blowing dust reduced visibilities in many areas of eastern New Mexico below 3 miles for several hours. Fortunately no significant damage was reported with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Mesonet site located northeast of Carrizozo recorded sustained winds in the lower to mid 40's with a peak gust up to 63 mph during the afternoon hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front approached the state from the Pacific Northwest and combined with a powerful jet stream aloft to produce widespread windy to very windy conditions. Westerly winds increased over western New Mexico ahead of the front. As the cold front pushed southeast across the state winds shifted to the northwest. Strong winds were reported over the central high terrain and adjacent east slopes and highlands. Winds gradually tapered off into the late morning hours Thursday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clines Corners ASOS recorded sustained wind speeds ranging fron 40-44mph with a peak wind gust to 63 mph overnight February 19 into the afternoon of February 20, 2014.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another very dry month with many locations not receiving any rain caused drought conditions to worsen across portions of Southwestern and Southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone was put into Severe(D2) drought on February 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry thunderstorms developed during the late morning hours of April 12, 2014 first across areas of northwestern New Mexico expanding eastward throughout the day. The shower activity produced little to no rain with virga present across much of the area. During the evening one of these dry showers created a virga bomb event which led to a 77 mph wind gust at Clines Corners.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clines Corners ASOS recorded a peak gust to 77 mph during the evening associated with virga from nearby dry thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front barreled south and west across the eastern plains during the late evening into overnight hours of January 26-27. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site UP363 - Matere in Quay County recorded high sustained winds speeds in the lower forties as a front moved through the area during the late evening into the overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture rich and very strong jet stream moved into the western United States, nosing into northern and central New Mexico Wednesday night January 29, into Thursday January 30, 2014. Upper level winds ranging from 140-160 knots helped to mix strong winds down to the surface along and east of the central mountain chain. The strongest winds initially impacted the higher ridges of the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains before spreading south and east. The focus for strong winds shifted to the south-central mountains and adjacent highlands into Friday, January 31.  Severe wind speeds were recorded across the state during this two day span with the ASOS at Raton airport recording a gust to 83 mph during the early morning hours of the 30th. ||Along with the severe wind the strong jet stream brought snow. As this deep fetch of Pacific moisture streamed across the state light rain and high terrain snow impacted northern border locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS located at Raton Crews Airport recorded gusts ranging from 69-83 mph during the early morning hours of January 30, 2013.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet stream over New Mexico combined with a deepening surface trough over the plains to produce another round of strong gusty winds.  The strongest winds were observed along ridges of the central mountain chain and adjacent east slopes extending eastward across the high plains.  A few virga showers that developed over central and western New Mexico also resulted in localized strong gusts at Gallup and Albuquerque where gust up to 61 mph were reported.   Several other areas reported peak wind gusts to around 60 mph, including Clines Corners, Clovis, Sumner Lake and White Sands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: White Sands Missile Range 616.
